dimension: Static schedule data for Istanbul rail (Metro İstanbul lines + Marmaray): GTFS feeds, first/last train times, operating hours, night metro, headways/frequencies, official timetables
summary: There IS an official GTFS-static feed covering Istanbul rail, published by İstanbul Büyükşehir Belediyesi (İBB) on its Open Data Portal as the "Toplu Ulaşım GTFS Verisi / Public Transport GTFS Data" dataset. It is distributed as individual GTFS CSV files (agency, calendar, frequencies, routes, shapes, stop_times, stops, trips) under the İBB Open Data License, free and no key. I downloaded and inspected the files: they include Metro İstanbul lines (M1A, M1B, M2, M2A, M3, M3A, M4, M5, M6, M7, M8, M9), trams (T1, T4), funicular (F3) and Marmaray (TCDD: GEBZE-HALKALI, plus Marmaray1/Marmaray2 branches), with real route_type=1/0/7 codes and a populated frequencies.txt carrying headway_secs. The critical caveat is freshness: the portal explicitly states "Bu veri güncellenmeyecektir / This data will not be updated," the per-source extracts are old (Metro İstanbul 2023-01-09, Marmaray/TCDD 2022-05-24), and the calendar service window ends 2024-12-31, so it is expired/static today (2026). For current numbers the authoritative sources are the per-line pages on metro.istanbul (Hatlarimiz/HatDetay) and the dynamic timetable tool (metro.istanbul/SeferDurumlari/SeferDetaylari), plus TCDD/Marmaray for the suburban line. Concrete figures: metro lines generally run 06:00-00:00; night metro ("Gece Metrosu") runs Fri 06:00 through Sun 00:00 on M1A/M1B/M2/M4/M5/M6/M7 with 30-min headways after 00:00 and double fare from 00:30 to 05:30; Marmaray runs ~05:30/06:00 to ~00:20 with peak 8-10 min and off-peak 15 min headways, extended Fri/Sat nights.

findings.claim: An official GTFS-static feed for Istanbul rail exists on the İBB Open Data Portal and includes both Metro İstanbul lines and Marmaray.
findings.detail: Dataset 'Toplu Ulaşım GTFS Verisi' / 'Public Transport GTFS Data' (dataset id 121a9892-7945-419a-9b89-49f6083926df). I downloaded routes.csv: it contains Marmaray (route_id 26615 'GEBZE-HALKALI', 26727 'SİĞİTLİÇEŞME-ZEYTİNBURNU' = Marmaray1, 28188 'HALKALI-BAHÇEŞEHİR' = Marmaray2; all agency_id 4 TCDD, route_type 1) and Metro İstanbul lines (agency_id 11): M1A(1296), M1B(1293), M2(1298), M2A(2044), M3(1297), M3A(2045), M4(1294), M5(7098), M6(3811), M7(28189), M8(28193), M9(28190), plus trams T1(4065)/T4(1289) route_type 0 and funicular F3(7237) route_type 7. agency.csv lists 'Metro İstanbul' (id 11) and 'TCDD' (id 4, marmaray.gov.tr).

findings.claim: The İBB rail GTFS feed is distributed as individual CSV files (not a single ZIP) with stable direct-download URLs, free and no API key required.
findings.detail: Direct download URLs (all under https://data.ibb.gov.tr/dataset/121a9892-7945-419a-9b89-49f6083926df/resource/.../download/): agency.csv (resource 42ae499d-ae9c-4906-ac5c-96e0c155e00b), calendar.csv (c84ca913-29ac-4f15-87cd-076aef3dccd6), frequencies.csv (a4c86ce6-64da-41e2-9584-5d83b5fb895c), routes.csv (36b554c7-cae0-4b7e-978f-fc6a43664e88), shapes.csv (83317085-aa56-41b0-9447-ea579567f2cb), stop_times.csv (ac646b83-3b6f-4ca2-afb4-9071ab44d9af), stops.csv (d1f7c258-bbc1-406f-9ab2-7a7c1797c673), trips.csv (dcee1700-e59f-4a5f-8009-f602045a4507). License: İBB Open Data License. I successfully curl-downloaded several of these.

findings.claim: The official İBB rail GTFS feed is stale / no longer maintained and its service calendar has expired.
findings.detail: The portal explicitly states 'Bu veri güncellenmeyecektir' / 'This data will not be updated.' Per-source extract dates (TR page): Metro İstanbul 09.01.2023, Marmaray (TCDD) 24.05.2022, Şehir Hatları 01.02.2023, others 2021-2022. I read calendar.csv directly: the main service_ids have start_date 20221231 and end_date 20241231, so the feed's service window ended 2024-12-31 and is expired for today's date (2026-06-27). Treat it as a structural/reference feed, not a live timetable.

findings.claim: The İBB GTFS feed contains a real frequencies.txt with headway data, and Marmaray is modeled frequency-based.
findings.detail: frequencies.csv header: trip_id,start_time,end_time,headway_secs,exact_times (2310 data rows). Verified Marmaray trips are frequency-based: trip 3059852 (Marmaray GEBZE) 06:00-24:00 headway 900s (15 min); trip 3071646 (Marmaray1 leg) 05:50-23:50 headway 480s (8 min); 3071645 05:50-23:54 headway 480s. Many metro trips (e.g. M2 route 1298 has 1368 trips) are full scheduled stop_times rather than frequency-based. Example metro headways in freq.csv: 1200s (20? no -> 20 min spacing values appear e.g. 1200=20min, 900=15min, 1800=30min, 480=8min).

findings.claim: A separate İETT GTFS dataset exists (buses only, not rail) and is also marked static.
findings.detail: Dataset 'İETT GTFS Verisi' (id 8540e256-6df5-4719-85bc-e64e91508ede) provides agency/calendar/routes/stop_times/stops/trips CSVs plus a stop_times.zip. It is also flagged 'This data will not be updated' (portal page showed a 2026-04-21 listing date but the data itself is static and covers İETT bus services only, not metro/Marmaray). Use the Public Transport GTFS dataset for rail, not this one.

findings.claim: Standard Metro İstanbul daily operating hours are 06:00 to 00:00 (midnight) on all lines, same on weekdays and weekends for the regular service.
findings.detail: Confirmed on official per-line pages: M2 '06.00 - 00.00', M4 first 06:00 / last 00:00, M7 06:00-00:00. News sources corroborate first train 06:00, last train 00:00 for all lines, with the same hours applied Fri/Sat/Sun and public holidays. The dynamic official tool metro.istanbul/SeferDurumlari/SeferDetaylari lets you query exact first/last train per station (M1A,M1B,M2,M3,M4,M5,M6,M7,M8,M9,T1,T3,T4,T5,F1,F4,TF1,TF2).

findings.claim: Night Metro ('Gece Metrosu') runs continuously from Friday 06:00 to Sunday 00:00 on seven lines, with 30-minute headways after midnight and double fare 00:30-05:30.
findings.detail: Official Gece-Metrosu page: continuous operation Friday 06:00 through Sunday 00:00 ('66 hours uninterrupted'), on nights connecting Fri->Sat and Sat->Sun, on lines M1A, M1B, M2 (except Sanayi-Seyrantepe branch), M4, M5, M6, M7. After 00:00 the interval is extended to 30 minutes. Double-fare tariff applies from 00:30 to 05:30. Service launched 30 Aug 2019 (M1A,M1B,M2,M4,M5,M6); M7 added 28 Oct 2020. Note: some general-news sources list six lines (omitting M7) and use older termini (e.g. M4 'Kadıköy-Tavşantepe', M5 'Üsküdar-Çekmeköy'); the official page is authoritative.

findings.claim: Per-line peak service frequencies (sefer sıklığı) from official Metro İstanbul line pages.
findings.detail: M2 (Yenikapı-Hacıosman): main section 3 min 55 sec all day; Sanayi Mahallesi-Seyrantepe shuttle 8.5 min. Length 23.49 km, 16 stations, 32 min end-to-end. M4 (Kadıköy-SAW/Tavşantepe): peak 5 min; length 33.5 km, 23 stations, 52 min, 386 trips/day one-way. M7 (Yıldız/Mecidiyeköy-Mahmutbey): peak 4 min; length ~20 km, 17 stations, 36 min; driverless. M1A peak ~6 min and M1B peak ~4 min (per news sources). These are official current figures and supersede the expired GTFS feed.

findings.claim: Marmaray first/last train times and frequencies.
findings.detail: First train ~05:30 (one source 05:58 from Halkalı / 06:05 from Gebze); last regular train ~23:20-00:20. Frequency: peak (approx 07:00-10:00 and 16:00-20:00) 8-10 min, with the busy Ataköy/Bostancı-Pendik core ~8 min; off-peak 15 min; late night up to 20-30 min. Weekend (Fri & Sat nights) extended: after the regular last departure there are additional 30-min-interval services; e.g. last Gebze departure 01:20 arriving Halkalı 03:08, last Halkalı departure 01:28 arriving Gebze 03:16. Operator is TCDD Taşımacılık; the official daily timetable page is tcddtasimacilik.gov.tr/marmaray (JS-rendered, not scrapeable as static HTML).

findings.claim: M11 (Gayrettepe - İstanbul Airport - Halkalı/Kağıthane) hours and frequency; note M11 is NOT in the İBB GTFS feed (too new / 2023+ opening).
findings.detail: M11 runs 06:00 to ~00:40, weekdays and weekends; Fri/Sat additional night services every 30 min after ~00:01 until ~05:30. Headway ~20 min (Gayrettepe-Arnavutköy Hastane), denser at peaks; Gayrettepe to İstanbul Airport ~31 min / 6 stops. M11 was absent from the routes.csv I downloaded, consistent with the feed predating/not covering the line's expansions.

findings.claim: The İBB GTFS data is mirrored on third-party open-data catalogs, but I could not confirm a fresh, maintained Istanbul rail feed on global aggregators (Transitland/MobilityDatabase) via direct retrieval.
findings.detail: The same datasets appear on ulasav.csb.gov.tr (Turkish Ministry/Çevre, e.g. /dataset/34-public-transport-gtfs-data) and opendata.b40cities.org as mirrors of İBB. MobilityDatabase (mobilitydatabase.org/feeds) and Transitland (transit.land) catalog global feeds, but I could not retrieve a confirmed Istanbul Metro/Marmaray feed entry: Transitland's feeds/operators pages are JS-rendered and returned no Turkish entries in static HTML, and its REST API requires an apikey (returned HTTP 401). So: aggregator availability is unconfirmed; the canonical source remains data.ibb.gov.tr.

findings.claim: Official timetable interfaces (not PDFs) are the live source of truth for per-line per-station first/last train and frequency.
findings.detail: Metro İstanbul: dynamic schedule tool at metro.istanbul/SeferDurumlari/SeferDetaylari (select line/station, query 'first/last train', now/later) and per-line pages metro.istanbul/Hatlarimiz/HatDetay?hat=<LINE> (e.g. =M2). Marmaray/TCDD: tcddtasimacilik.gov.tr/marmaray/tr/gunluk_tren_saatleri. These are JS apps rather than static PDFs; I did not find downloadable official timetable PDFs per line.

Does Metro İstanbul or İBB expose a GTFS-Realtime feed (vehicle positions / trip updates) for 'anlık tren konumu'? Not found in this pass; the IBB portal also offers a separate real-time/SOAP/IETT API ecosystem worth checking (e.g. IETT SOAP/RestAPI, api.ibb.gov.tr).

Newer/expansion lines (M11 Gayrettepe-İstanbul Airport-Halkalı, M4 extension to Tavşantepe/Sabiha Gökçen, M5 to Sultanbeyli, M7 to Esenyurt, M8 Bostancı-Parseller, M9, M12) are missing or outdated in the expired GTFS feed; current line termini and timetables must be taken from metro.istanbul.

dimension: Geographic data for Istanbul rail: station coordinates and line geometries (track paths)
summary: The single best authoritative source is the İBB Open Data Portal (data.ibb.gov.tr), which publishes three official WGS84 GeoJSON datasets maintained by the Rail Systems Department: station POINTS (343 features), line GEOMETRIES (37 MultiLineString features with rich per-line attributes), and station AREAS (polygons). Crucially, both files carry a PROJE_ASAMA (project phase) field that cleanly separates existing stations/lines from under-construction ones, and a HAT_TURU/PROJE_TURU type field (Metro/Tramvay/Banliyö(Marmaray)/Füniküler/Teleferik). For an alternative, OpenStreetMap is comprehensive and well-tagged: every line has route relations (network="İstanbul Metrosu" for metro/tram/funicular, network="Marmaray" for commuter rail) plus route_master relations, and under-construction lines (M12, M13, M20, M34/Hızray, extensions) ARE present. I retrieved concrete OSM relation IDs and ~179 metro/light_rail station nodes via the Overpass API. Wikidata (SPARQL) also returns station coordinates with line membership. A ready-made community GitHub dataset (Hero4mohamed/Metro-Istanbul-General-City-Map) bundles 23 line polylines + named station coordinates + a planned-lines file, but has NO license. The legacy ustroetz gist (2014, Mapzen) is outdated.

findings.claim: İBB Open Data Portal publishes an official station POINTS GeoJSON with 343 rail station features in WGS84, distinguishing existing vs under-construction stations.
findings.detail: Dataset slug 'rayli-sistem-istasyon-noktalari-verisi'. Direct download: https://data.ibb.gov.tr/dataset/04ec9805-2483-46c7-914f-30c50857a846/resource/3dc8203f-3613-48a8-85e9-24fffb7821ad/download/rayli_sistem_istasyon_poi_verisi.geojson . I downloaded and parsed it: 343 Point features. Properties per feature: ISTASYON (station name), PROJE_ADI (line/project name e.g. 'T1 Kabataş - Bağcılar Tramvay Hattı'), HAT_TURU (line type), PROJE_ASAMA (phase), MUDURLUK (operating dept). Coordinates are [lon, lat] WGS84 (e.g. [28.881680707, 41.0058001386]). HAT_TURU breakdown: Metro 213, Tramvay 77, Banliyö(Marmaray/commuter) 43, Füniküler 6, Teleferik(cable car) 4. PROJE_ASAMA breakdown: 'Mevcut Hattaki İstasyon' (existing) 268, 'İnşaat Aşamasında' (under construction) 75. Free, open, no key required.

findings.claim: İBB also publishes an official line GEOMETRIES GeoJSON (37 MultiLineString features) WITH rich per-line attributes including type, length, and project phase.
findings.detail: Dataset slug 'rayli-ulasim-hatlari-vektor-verisi'. Direct download: https://data.ibb.gov.tr/dataset/8b8603dd-2642-4789-a891-4bb7cb2c94e8/resource/fe4ec165-9d11-4b83-b031-caea3cfaae55/download/rayli_sistem_hat_verisi.geojson . I downloaded and parsed it: 37 MultiLineString features (NOT geometry-only — an automated summarizer initially misreported it). Properties: PROJE_TURU (type), PROJE_AD_KISA / PROJE_ADI (line name), KAPASITE, UZUNLUK (length km), PROJE_ASAMA (phase), MUDURLUK. PROJE_TURU breakdown: Metro 23, Tramvay 4, Füniküler 3, Nostaljik Tramvay 2, Teleferik 2, Hafif Metro 2, Banliyö 1. PROJE_ASAMA: Mevcut(existing) 24, İnşaat Aşamasında(under construction) 12, Marmaray 1. Covers M1A,M1B,M2,M3,M4,M5,M6,M7(+extensions),M8,M9,M11,M12, T1-T5, F1,F2,F4, teleferiks, Marmaray, plus future lines (Pendik-SGH, Kaynarca-Pendik-Tuzla, Sirkeci-Kazlıçeşme, Altunizade-Çamlıca). Free, open, no key. Note: file is valid UTF-8 (Turkish chars display as mojibake only in non-UTF8 consoles).

findings.claim: İBB publishes a third official dataset: rail station AREAS (polygons) GeoJSON.
findings.detail: Dataset slug 'rayli-ulasim-istasyonlari-vektor-verisi'. Direct download: https://data.ibb.gov.tr/dataset/271c7bdf-4b0c-4642-8e50-96acba3d4756/resource/3804f1c6-10de-4fa0-9753-74e5df485d3a/download/rayli_sistem_istasyon_verisi.geojson . Useful when you need station footprints/areas rather than centroid points. Free, open, no key. The portal is CKAN-based; you can enumerate all rail datasets via the CKAN API: https://data.ibb.gov.tr/api/3/action/package_search?q=rayl%C4%B1+sistem+istasyon

findings.claim: OpenStreetMap has complete, well-tagged route relations for all Istanbul metro/tram/funicular lines under network='İstanbul Metrosu', queryable via Overpass; I obtained concrete relation IDs.
Marmaray (commuter rail) is mapped in OSM as type=route, route=train, service=commuter, network='Marmaray', operator='TCDD Taşımacılık', ref=B1.
findings.detail: Two direction relations verified directly on osm.org: Gebze→Halkalı = relation 9468040 (139 members; tags type=route, route=train, ref=B1, name='Marmaray: Gebze - Halkalı', network=Marmaray, operator='TCDD Taşımacılık', service=commuter); Halkalı→Gebze = relation 9987139. Marmaray stations are mapped: an Overpass query for node[railway=station][network=Marmaray] returned 28 station nodes; the full 43-station line includes additional stations shared/tagged under other variants. So Marmaray IS mapped (both geometry and stations).

findings.claim: Under-construction lines ARE present in OSM, captured as route_master relations.
findings.detail: Overpass query relation[type=route_master][network~Metro] in the Istanbul bbox returned future/under-construction masters: M7 Mahmutbey-Esenyurt (13895537), M12 Göztepe-Ümraniye (15520693), M13 Söğütlüçeşme-Yenidoğan (15547419), M7 Yıldız-Kabataş (15547431), M20 İncirli-Beylikdüzü (15547627), M1B Kirazlı-Halkalı (18690036), M34 'Hızray' (18690177), M2 Yenikapı-İncirli (18690178). This confirms OSM coverage of planned/under-construction segments, complementing İBB's PROJE_ASAMA='İnşaat Aşamasında' flag.

findings.claim: OSM contains ~179 metro/light_rail station nodes for Istanbul, retrievable with a station-node Overpass query.
findings.detail: Query: node[railway=station][station~'subway|light_rail'] OR node[railway=station][subway=yes] in bbox (40.7,28.3,41.4,29.6) returned count=179. For full coverage also query railway=station with the route relations' member stops. Station nodes typically carry name, name:tr, ref, network, operator, and wikidata tags.

findings.claim: A ready-made community GitHub dataset bundles all Istanbul line polylines + named station coordinates + planned lines, but it has NO license file.
findings.detail: Repo: Hero4mohamed/Metro-Istanbul-General-City-Map (https://github.com/Hero4mohamed/Metro-Istanbul-General-City-Map). File transit_data/lines.json = JSON array of 23 lines, each {ref, kind(subway/tram/funicular/marmaray/metrobus), color(hex), paths(array of [lat,lng] polylines), stations(array of {name,lat,lng}), branch, partOf, scope}. Refs: M1A,M1B,M2,M2S,M3,M4,M5,M6,M7,M8,M9,M11,Marmaray,T1-T5,F1-F4,Metrobüs. transit_data/planned-lines.json = 6 under-construction lines (M7X,M4X,M10,M12,T6,T7) with status/launch/official fields. Also ferry-lines.json, b2-line.json, disruptions.json. CAUTION: no LICENSE file in the repo (GitHub /license API returns 404) — reuse rights unclear; treat as unofficial. Note [lat,lng] order here vs İBB/OSM [lon,lat].

findings.claim: Wikidata holds Istanbul metro station coordinates with line membership, queryable via SPARQL.
findings.detail: Istanbul Metro = Q498172. A SPARQL query (?station wdt:P31/wdt:P279* wd:Q928830 [metro station]; wdt:P16 [transport network] ?line; ?line wdt:P361* wd:Q498172; wdt:P625 ?coord) returns stations with P625 coordinate values and labels (e.g. Taksim Point(28.985981928 41.037075959), Gayrettepe (M11), Levent (M6), Kozyatağı (M8), Yenisahra (M12)). Endpoint: https://query.wikidata.org/sparql . Free, no key. Good as a cross-check / for wikipedia links; coverage is decent but less authoritative/complete than İBB or OSM, and broad COUNT queries time out (use line-scoped queries).

findings.claim: The frequently-cited ustroetz GitHub gist is outdated and not recommended as a primary source.
findings.detail: https://gist.github.com/ustroetz/72fd91c7a970049f22e1 contains public_stop.geojson with 706 features (railway stations 112, subway entrances 60, tram stops 46, plus bus stops) but is a Mapzen Istanbul Metro Extract dated 19 Nov 2014 — predates M5,M7,M8,M9,M11,Marmaray full line, etc. Use only for historical reference.

findings.claim: geojsons-of-turkey repo does NOT contain rail data (admin borders only).
findings.detail: izzetkalic/geojsons-of-turkey (https://github.com/izzetkalic/geojsons-of-turkey) — I listed the full file tree; it has only administrative boundary GeoJSONs (admin-level-2/4/6/8, neighbourhoods). No metro/rail/tram/station files. Useful only for district/neighbourhood overlays, not rail geometry.

dataSources.name: İBB Rail Station POINTS GeoJSON (rayli_sistem_istasyon_poi_verisi)

dimension: Operational characteristics for simulating Istanbul rail train positions (inter-station run times, dwell, turnaround, commercial speeds, shared-track/interlining, intraday frequency, operating rules)
summary: Istanbul rail can be simulated to high fidelity from two free, no-key data sources: (1) the IBB static GTFS feed (data.ibb.gov.tr), whose stop_times.csv gives per-segment scheduled run times AND dwell for every metro/Marmaray/tram trip, plus frequencies.csv for night/supplemental headways; and (2) the live, key-free official Metro Istanbul Mobile API (api.ibb.gov.tr/MetroIstanbul) which returns lines (with colors, first/last times), full station lists with order + lat/lon, and a POST GetTimeTable endpoint for actual scheduled times. I downloaded and analyzed the GTFS directly: dwell is modeled as a uniform 15 s at all intermediate stations (a simplification, not measured), while inter-station run times are realistic and vary 45-285 s; modeled interstation cruise speed is ~45 km/h and end-to-end commercial speed is ~39-41 km/h across all rail modes. The M1A/M1B shared trunk (Yenikapi-Otogar) runs a combined ~3-min headway with each branch at ~6 min (classic interlining); Marmaray runs full-line Gebze-Halkali at 15-min headway with an overlaid inner Ataköy/Zeytinburnu-Pendik short-turn pattern at ~8 min, giving B1/B2-style service. Real-world dwell is ~20-60 s on metro and 60-120 s on Marmaray per secondary sources; terminal turnaround is not published and should be estimated at 3-6 min (recovery/layover ~10-20% of run time) using standard transit-sim practice. The GTFS is a static historical snapshot ("will not be updated"; files Last-Modified 2024-03) and there is no public GTFS-Realtime / live vehicle-position feed for Istanbul rail, so positions must be simulated by interpolating along the schedule/shape, not read live.

findings.claim: IBB publishes a free, no-login static GTFS feed covering Metro Istanbul, Marmaray (TCDD) and trams, with direct CSV download URLs.
findings.detail: Dataset 'Public Transport GTFS Data' (package id 121a9892-7945-419a-9b89-49f6083926df) exposes 8 CSV resources via the CKAN API. Verified direct downloads (HTTP 200): stop_times.csv (https://data.ibb.gov.tr/dataset/121a9892-7945-419a-9b89-49f6083926df/resource/ac646b83-3b6f-4ca2-afb4-9071ab44d9af/download/stop_times.csv, ~11.8 MB, 199,981 rows), trips.csv (.../dcee1700-e59f-4a5f-8009-f602045a4507/...), frequencies.csv (.../a4c86ce6-64da-41e2-9584-5d83b5fb895c/...), routes.csv (.../36b554c7-cae0-4b7e-978f-fc6a43664e88/...), stops.csv (.../d1f7c258-bbc1-406f-9ab2-7a7c1797c673/...), shapes.csv, calendar.csv, agency.csv. No API key/login required. Files use Windows-1254 (Turkish) encoding. route_type=1 marks metro+Marmaray, route_type=0 marks trams. Rail route_ids: M1A=1296, M1B=1293, M2=1298, M3=1297, M4=1294, M5=7098, M6=3811, M7=28189, M8=28193, M9=28190, Marmaray full=26615, Marmaray1(B1-style)=26727, Marmaray2(B2-style)=28188, T1=4065.

findings.claim: GTFS stop_times.csv contains both arrival_time and departure_time plus cumulative shape_dist_traveled per stop, so exact per-segment run times, dwell, and distances can be computed directly for every rail line.
findings.detail: I parsed stop_times.csv and computed, for a representative full-length trip per line, run time = arrival[n] - departure[n-1], dwell = departure - arrival, segment distance = delta shape_dist_traveled (km), and segment speed. Worked examples: M2 (Yenikapi-Haciosman, 15 stops) total 29.1 min over 19.01 km, segment runs 68-176 s, interstation speeds ~45 km/h after the first (acceleration) segment, commercial speed 39.2 km/h. M4 (19 stops in this snapshot) 38.4 min / 25.24 km / 39.4 km/h. Marmaray full (43 stops) Halkali->Gebze 111 min / 75.61 km / 40.7 km/h, segment runs 60-309 s. Across lines: metro run-time means ~85-145 s/segment; Marmaray ~145-225 s/segment.

findings.claim: Dwell time in the GTFS is a uniform modeling constant of 15 seconds at all intermediate stations, not a measured value; real-world dwell is longer.
findings.detail: Every intermediate stop in every rail trip I checked has departure_time = arrival_time + 15 s exactly (dwell set = {15} for M1A,M1B,M2,M3,M4,M5,M6,M7,M8,M9,Marmaray,T1,T3,T4). This is clearly a scheduling placeholder. Secondary sources report real dwell of roughly 20-60 s on metro and '60 seconds to 2 minutes' on Marmaray. For simulation use ~20-30 s metro interior stations, 30-45 s at busy interchanges, 60-90 s Marmaray, and add extra at terminals.

findings.claim: A live, key-free official Metro Istanbul Mobile Web API exists and returns line, station (with order + coordinates), and timetable data — ideal authoritative input for a simulator.
findings.detail: Base: https://api.ibb.gov.tr/MetroIstanbul/api/MetroMobile/V2/. Verified live (HTTP 200, no key): GET GetLines returns 18 lines each with Id, Name, Color (RGB), FirstTime, LastTime, multilingual Content; GET GetStations returns every station with Id, Name, LineId, LineName, Order, FunctionalCode and DetailInfo.Latitude/Longitude. Example M4 in-order: Kadikoy (40.99066,29.02201) -> Ayrilik Cesmesi -> Acibadem -> Unalan -> Goztepe ... POST GetTimeTable(TimeTableRequest model) exists (returns 405 on GET, validates a JSON model on POST) for actual scheduled departure times. GetDirections also exists (HTTP 500 without params). Documented in the IBB open-data catalog 'Metro Istanbul Sefer Tarifeleri Listesi Web Servisi'. NOTE: the /MetroIstanbul/Help page and the exact GetTimeTable request schema are gated behind a browser/WAF check and I could not enumerate the POST field names blindly (every application/json body returned a parser error referencing an injected '<', while a bare scalar reached the model binder), so the precise TimeTableRequest property names remain unconfirmed.

findings.claim: M1A and M1B interline over a shared trunk Yenikapi-Otogar: combined ~3-min peak headway on the trunk, ~6-min on each branch.
findings.detail: Per metro.istanbul official line pages, both lines run 06:00-00:00 with 3-min peak headway on the Yenikapi-Otogar shared section and 6-min on the Otogar-Ataturk Airport (M1A) and Otogar-Kirazli (M1B) branches; trains alternate at Otogar where the line splits. M1A end-to-end ~35 min, M1B ~25 min. For simulation: generate trains on the trunk at 3-min spacing and alternate their branch assignment at the Otogar junction. (Note: M1A's Ataturk Airport terminus is the legacy alignment; service patterns have changed since airport closure, so validate against the live GetTimeTable.)

findings.claim: Marmaray runs a full-line pattern plus an overlaid inner short-turn pattern (the B1/B2-style service): full Gebze-Halkali at 15-min headway, inner Atakoy/Zeytinburnu-Pendik at ~8 min.
findings.detail: Published service: Gebze-Halkali every 15 min; the busy central section (Atakoy-Pendik) every 8 min thanks to extra short-turn trains, so the core gets ~combined higher frequency. GTFS confirms this structure: route 26615 (full) carries 15-min (900 s) frequency bands all day, while route 26727 (inner pattern, sampled Zeytinburnu<->Sogutlucesme) carries 8-min (480 s) frequency bands 05:50-23:54. 43 stations, 76.6 km, full run 108-115 min (GTFS-computed 111 min), commercial speed ~41 km/h, ~4 min under the Bosphorus. First/last: Gebze first 06:05/last 22:50, Halkali first 05:58/last 22:58. Simulation: run two overlaid train generators (full + inner short-turn) sharing the same track between Atakoy/Zeytinburnu and Pendik.

findings.claim: Intraday frequency: most daytime trips are individually scheduled (explicit stop_times); frequencies.csv mainly encodes night/early/late supplemental service. Driverless lines show a clean peak/shoulder/night profile.
findings.detail: frequencies.csv example M7: 06:00-06:45 = 480 s (8 min), 06:45-22:00 = 360 s (6 min peak/base), 22:00-24:00 = 480 s (8 min), 00:20-06:00 = 1200 s (20 min night). Metro lines M1A/M2/M4 etc. have frequency bands only for the 23:50-02:00 night window (600 s = 10 min) because daytime service is enumerated as discrete trips. Practical simulation approach: drive train spawning from per-period headways (peak ~3-6 min, base ~5-8 min, evening ~8-10 min, late-night ~10-20 min) and add/remove trains at period boundaries by injecting/retiring runs at terminals.

findings.claim: Commercial speeds by mode: heavy metro and Marmaray both ~39-41 km/h end-to-end; trams much slower (T3 ~10.6 min/10 stops). M11 is the outlier high-speed metro at up to 120 km/h.
findings.detail: GTFS-computed commercial speeds: M2 39.2, M4 39.4, M5 ~ (26.6 min/26.5 km), Marmaray 40-41 km/h. Interstation cruise modeled at ~45 km/h with slower first/short segments. Trams (T1,T3,T4) run mixed-traffic-like timings, much lower commercial speed. M11 (Gayrettepe-Istanbul Airport-Halkali, 69 km, 16 stations) is designed for 120 km/h max operating speed ('Turkey's first high-speed metro'); all other lines max ~80 km/h. Estimation fallback when no schedule: heavy metro ~32-40 km/h commercial / ~80 km/h max; LRT/tram ~18-25 km/h; Marmaray ~40-45 km/h.

findings.claim: Per-line official first/last train times and brand colors are available from the live API for accurate service-window simulation.
findings.detail: From GetLines: M2 00:20-23:59, M4 00:30-23:59, M5 00:30-00:00, M6 00:30-23:59, M7 00:30-00:00, M9 06:00-23:59, M1A 00:35-00:00, M1B 00:35-00:02 (the 00:xx 'first' values reflect weekend all-night metro service), M3 06:00-00:00, M8 06:05-00:00, T1 06:00-23:36, T3 08:30-21:00, F1/F4 funiculars 06:00-00:00. Colors as RGB e.g. M2 (0,153,68) green, M4 (233,30,118) pink, M1A/M1B (238,49,36) red, M7 (248,154,186). Weekend overnight ('gece metrosu') service runs continuously Fri 06:00 through Sun 00:00 on selected lines.

findings.claim: No public GTFS-Realtime / live vehicle-position feed exists for Istanbul rail; positions must be simulated, and the static GTFS is explicitly frozen.
findings.detail: The GTFS dataset page states 'This data will not be updated' (resource files Last-Modified 2024-03-13). No Istanbul rail GTFS-RT feed found on Mobility Database or Transitland. A community live map (Hero4mohamed/Metro-Istanbul-General-City-Map / 'Ray-Net') confirms the standard approach: it draws OSM-derived line geometry (via Overpass API) and shows 'animated live carriages with station dwell + predictive arrivals' — i.e. schedule-based interpolation, NOT GPS tracking. A previously-referenced TypeScript wrapper (github ardytstrn/16-96 metro.istanbul) now 404s. Recommended simulation method: place trains by interpolating along the route shape using GTFS/live-API scheduled arrival/departure times (or synthesized headways), applying realistic acceleration on first/short segments, modeled dwell, and terminal turnaround.

findings.claim: Terminal turnaround/layover is not published by Metro Istanbul; use standard transit-sim estimates of ~3-6 minutes (recovery time ~10-20% of one-way run).
findings.detail: No official Istanbul figure found. General rail-operations practice: terminal layover/recovery is typically 10-20% of total running time, often 3-6 min at metro termini (driverless lines can reverse faster with scissors crossovers/turnback tracks). For a line with ~30 min run time, 4-5 min turnaround per end is realistic; this also sets the minimum cycle time and thus how many trains are needed for a given headway (fleet = ceil(round-trip cycle time / headway)).

findings.claim: Simple fallback for inter-station times when no schedule is available: app calculators use a flat ~2 minutes per station; better is distance/commercial-speed plus a fixed stop penalty.
findings.detail: metrodakikahesapla.com presents cumulative travel time as a flat ~2 min between adjacent stations (e.g. M1B Yenikapi 0 -> Aksaray 2 -> ... -> Kirazli 24 min) and states 'Ortalama Duraklar Arasi Sure: 2 dakika'. More accurate estimation: run_time = segment_distance / cruise_speed (use ~45 km/h interstation for metro, lower for trams) + an acceleration/deceleration penalty (~10-20 s), then add dwell. Distances come from GTFS shape_dist_traveled or from station lat/lon (GetStations) along the OSM shape.

dataSources.notes: Marmaray operator (TCDD) page; primary source for Marmaray service though dynamic content limited via scrape.

openQuestions: Exact JSON schema (property names) of the GetTimeTable POST body (TimeTableRequest) and the meaning of Direction/Day type codes — blocked by a WAF/browser gate on /MetroIstanbul/Help; needs a browser session or captured mobile-app request to confirm.

Real measured dwell times per station/line (the GTFS 15 s is a placeholder). Could be derived by sampling live GetTimeTable arrival/departure deltas at interchange vs interior stations, or from academic studies of Istanbul metro dwell.

Official terminal turnaround/layover times per line (not published); currently estimated. Could be inferred from live timetables (gap between a train's last arrival and its next first departure at the terminus).

How fresh the live GetLines/GetStations data is for recently changed services (e.g. M1A after Ataturk Airport closure, M3/M9 extensions, M5 to Sultanbeyli). The static GTFS snapshot lags; the live API should be treated as source of truth and re-pulled.

Whether any line uses skip-stop / express patterns or scheduled short-turns beyond Marmaray's inner pattern and M1A/M1B branching — none found in sources reviewed; would need to inspect full GetTimeTable/stop_times per direction to confirm.
Confirmed M11 (and newer lines M12, etc.) operational run times and headways — M11 appears in the live GetLines set's siblings but detailed segment timing was not extracted in this pass.

dimension: Complete 2026 inventory of Istanbul rail lines and stations (cross-checked against Metro İstanbul official Railway Network Map v.3 rev.20.0, June 2026)
summary: I built a complete current (June 2026) inventory of Istanbul's rail network, cross-checking the official Metro İstanbul "İstanbul Raylı Sistemler Ağ Haritası" PDF, whose footer confirms the exact edition: "v.3 rev.20.0 (Haziran 2026)" and "© Metro İstanbul 2026". As of June 2026 there are 11 metro lines (M1A, M1B, M2, M3, M4, M5, M6, M7, M8, M9, M11) in operation; 5 tram lines (T1, T3, T4, T5, plus T6 Sirkeci-Kazlıçeşme operated under TCDD) and the nostalgic T2 Taksim-Tünel; funiculars F1 (Taksim-Kabataş), F3 (Seyrantepe-Vadistanbul), F4 (Hisarüstü-Aşiyan) and the heritage Tünel (coded F2 by İETT, Karaköy-Beyoğlu); cable cars TF1 (Maçka-Taşkışla) and TF2 (Eyüp-Piyer Loti); and Marmaray (Halkalı-Gebze, 43 stations, TCDD Taşımacılık, with B1/B2 service patterns). A major 2026 change: M11's Halkalı extension opened 20 June 2026, so M11 now runs the full Gayrettepe-İstanbul Airport-Halkalı (16 stations). The most authoritative source for per-line canonical station ordering is the official Metro İstanbul HatDetay pages (one per line); the network map PDF is authoritative for the line roster, transfers and colors but its text extraction is spatially jumbled so I relied on per-line pages for ordering. Lines under construction/planned for the data model include M10, M12, M13, M14, the M1B Kirazlı-Halkalı extension, M4 Tavşantepe-Tuzla, M7 Kabataş-Yıldız and Mahmutbey-Esenyurt extensions, and tram T7. Metrobüs (BRT) appears on the map but is a separate bus rapid transit system, not rail.

findings.claim: The canonical network map is Metro İstanbul 'İstanbul Raylı Sistemler Ağ Haritası / Istanbul Railway Network Map', edition v.3 rev.20.0, dated Haziran (June) 2026.
findings.detail: The official PDF footer reads exactly 'v.3 rev.20.0 (Haziran 2026)' and '© Metro İstanbul 2026 - Tüm Hakları Saklıdır. - All Rights Reserved.' This is the live map served from the metro.istanbul Ağ Haritaları page. It is the authoritative roster for which lines exist, line names, colors and transfer points. Downloadable PDF (free, no key).

findings.claim: M1A Yenikapı-Atatürk Havalimanı: 18 stations, red (#EE2229).
findings.detail: Ordered: Yenikapı, Aksaray, Emniyet-Fatih, Topkapı-Ulubatlı, Bayrampaşa-Maltepe, Sağmalcılar, Kocatepe, Otogar, Terazidere, Davutpaşa-YTÜ, Merter, Zeytinburnu, Bakırköy-İncirli, Bahçelievler, Ataköy-Şirinevler, Yenibosna, DTM-İstanbul Fuar Merkezi, Atatürk Havalimanı. Source for ordering: official Metro İstanbul HatDetay page (M1A). Opened 1989.

findings.claim: M1B Yenikapı-Kirazlı: 13 stations, red (#EE2229), shares trunk with M1A from Yenikapı to Otogar.
findings.detail: Branches from the M1A trunk at Otogar toward Kirazlı. Trunk stations Yenikapı...Otogar shared with M1A; M1B-specific tail includes Esenler, Menderes, Üçyüzlü, Bağcılar, Kirazlı-Bağcılar. The official HatDetay?hat=M1B page is the canonical ordering source (it loaded successfully in search). Opened 2013.

findings.claim: M2 Yenikapı-Hacıosman (with Seyrantepe branch): 16 stations counting Seyrantepe (15 main line + Seyrantepe spur), green (#059A4D).
findings.detail: Main line ordered: Yenikapı, Vezneciler-İstanbul Ü., Haliç, Şişhane, Taksim, Osmanbey, Şişli-Mecidiyeköy, Gayrettepe, Levent, 4.Levent, Sanayi Mahallesi, İTÜ-Ayazağa, Atatürk Oto Sanayi, Darüşşafaka, Hacıosman. Seyrantepe is a branch reached by transfer at Sanayi Mahallesi (shuttle). Sources differ on count: official page lists 16 (incl. Seyrantepe); tr.wikipedia summary says 15 main. Opened 2000.

findings.claim: M3 Bakırköy Sahil-Kayaşehir Merkez: 20 stations, light blue (#0CA6DF), with a Başakşehir/Metrokent branch toward Olimpiyat in some configurations.
findings.detail: Ordered (official HatDetay): Bakırköy Sahil, Özgürlük Meydanı, İncirli, Haznedar, İlkyuva, Yıldıztepe, Molla Gürani, Kirazlı-Bağcılar, Yenimahalle, Mahmutbey, İSTOÇ, İkitelli Sanayi, Turgut Özal, Siteler, Başak Konutları, Başakşehir-Metrokent, Onurkent, Şehir Hastanesi, Toplu Konutlar, Kayaşehir Merkez. Note: a Başakşehir-Metrokent to Olimpiyat branch historically existed; current map shows the Kayaşehir trunk. Opened 2013, extended to Kayaşehir Mar 2024.

findings.claim: M4 Kadıköy-Sabiha Gökçen Havalimanı: 23 stations, pink/magenta (#E81E77).

The map shows western stations such as Bahçeşehir, Ispartakule, Esenyurt area. This indicates a TCDD suburban service west of Halkalı toward Bahçeşehir/Çerkezköy corridor; treat as a separate line code (B2) in the data model.

findings.claim: Key transfer/interchange stations (aktarma istasyonları) and the lines that meet.
findings.detail: Yenikapı: M1A + M1B + M2 + Marmaray (B1). Gayrettepe: M2 + M11. Şişli-Mecidiyeköy (M2) <-> Mecidiyeköy (M7) <-> Metrobüs. Kirazlı-Bağcılar: M1B + M3. Mahmutbey: M3 + M7. Üsküdar: M5 + Marmaray. Ayrılık Çeşmesi (M4) <-> Ayrılık Çeşmesi (Marmaray) and adjacent Kadıköy ferries. Kadıköy: M4 + T3 + ferries. Bostancı: M4 + M8 + Marmaray. Kozyatağı: M4 + M8. Ünalan: M4 + M8 (via Ataşehir area). Kağıthane: M7 + M11. Alibeyköy: M7 + T5. Kabataş: T1 + F1 (+ ferries). Taksim: M2 + F1 + T2. Sanayi Mahallesi: M2 main <-> M2 Seyrantepe branch <-> F3 (via Seyrantepe). Levent: M2 + M6. Boğaziçi Ü./Hisarüstü: M6 + F4. Eminönü/Sirkeci: T1 + T5/T6 + Marmaray (Sirkeci) + ferries. Zeytinburnu: M1A + T1. Bakırköy area: M3 (İncirli) + M1A (Bakırköy-İncirli) nearby; Ataköy: M9 + Marmaray. Yenibosna: M1A + M9. Ataköy-Şirinevler/Yenibosna cluster links M1A and M9. Pendik: M4 + Marmaray (nearby). Kartal: M4 + Marmaray (nearby). Söğütlüçeşme: Marmaray + M4 (via Ayrılık Çeşmesi). The official network map is the authoritative source for transfer symbols ('Aktarma İşaretleri').

findings.claim: Lines UNDER CONSTRUCTION (per Metro İstanbul 'Proje/İnşa Halindeki Hatlar' and the map's 'Yapımı Devam Eden Hatlar' legend).
findings.detail: M12 Göztepe-Ümraniye-Kazım Karabekir (driverless, ~13 km, 11-12 stations, termini 60. Yıl Parkı <-> Kazım Karabekir; target opening end-2026). M13 Emek-Yenidoğan (map legend 'Emek-Yenidoğan Metro Hattı'; broader Yenidoğan-Söğütlüçeşme corridor). M14 Altunizade-Bosna Bulvarı (~4-4.5 km, 4 stations). M10 Pendik-SGH, Pendik Merkez-Fevzi Çakmak section. M1B Kirazlı(-Bağcılar)-Halkalı Üniversite extension. M4 Tavşantepe-Kaynarca Merkez / Tuzla extension. M7 Kabataş-Yıldız extension and M7 Mahmutbey-Esenyurt Meydanı extension. M2 Esenyurt Meydanı and Kabataş extensions referenced. T7 Eyüpsultan-Bayrampaşa tram line.

openQuestions: Official Metro İstanbul brand hex codes: not published in retrievable text form. The canonical colors are the swatches embedded in the official map PDF; the listed hex codes come from Wikipedia's template module and should be visually verified against the PDF (or a Metro İstanbul brand/style guide) before being treated as brand-exact.

Exact station ordering for M1B and M11 could not be pulled from the official HatDetay pages (M11 and F3 URLs returned 404; M1B not directly fetched in detail). M11/F3 orders here come from Wikipedia; M1B trunk-vs-branch split should be confirmed against the official M1B HatDetay page.

GTFS / real-time feed: this research targeted the static line/station inventory. Whether Metro İstanbul or İBB publish an official GTFS-static or GTFS-RT feed (e.g. on data.ibb.gov.tr / İBB Açık Veri) for live train positions was NOT investigated here and is the key open item for a 'live' app data model.
B2 Halkalı-Bahçeşehir suburban line: appears in the map legend but its full station list and current operational status (vs. under-construction toward Çerkezköy) needs a dedicated TCDD source check.

M2 station count ambiguity: official page counts 16 (incl. Seyrantepe branch) while some summaries say 15 main-line stations — confirm whether the data model should treat Seyrantepe as part of M2 or as a separate shuttle/branch.

M4 mid-section ordering (Göztepe / Yenisahra / Kozyatağı / Bostancı) and M8 'Kayışdağı' presence differ slightly across third-party sources; the official HatDetay ordering was used but should be re-verified against the map for the data model.

M12/M13/M14 final station names and exact counts are still moving targets (under construction); M12 listed as 11-12 stations across sources, M13 corridor named both 'Emek-Yenidoğan' (map) and 'Yenidoğan-Söğütlüçeşme' (Wikipedia).

dimension: Product analysis of the official Metro İstanbul mobile app and best-in-class transit apps (feature/UX research)
summary: The official "Metro İstanbul" app is an information-and-planning app, not a live-tracking app: it offers static published timetables (first/last train, scheduled departures), network maps, station finder/directions, fares, and news/announcements organized under five tabs (Nasıl Giderim, Sefer Tarifeleri, Ağ Haritaları, Hattınızı Seçin, Bilet & Ücretler). It does NOT show live train positions, next-train countdowns, real-time delays, crowding, or detailed accessibility/step-free routing. Ratings are weak (~2.67/5 on iOS, ~3.6/5 on Android third-party trackers) and reviews/complaint boards (Şikayetvar) cite crashes after line additions (e.g. M11), inaccurate arrival predictions, and poor real-time disruption communication. Crucially, İBB publishes static GTFS via data.ibb.gov.tr (covering Metro, Marmaray, IETT, ferries) and IETT's "Otobüsüm Nerede" (formerly Mobiett) already does live bus tracking, so the live-train gap is a product gap, not purely a data gap. Best-in-class apps (Citymapper, Transit, Moovit, TfL Go, the new MTA app, Trafi) point to clear, adoptable patterns for live vehicle visualization, GO-style trip navigation, carriage/crowding guidance, and step-free accessibility modes.

findings.claim: The official Metro İstanbul app's core feature set is information + static timetables, organized under five tabs, with no live train tracking.
findings.detail: Official Dec 2019 redesign announcement and store/aggregator descriptions list: Nasıl Giderim (trip/route help), Sefer Tarifeleri (timetables), Ağ Haritaları (network maps), Hattınızı Seçin (line selection), Bilet & Ücretler (fares); plus TR/EN support, nearest-station directions, zoomable station map, Istanbul Railway Network Map, current fares, news/events/announcements, and IBB BeyazMasa/contact links. The Sefer Tarifeleri tool serves static published schedules (first/last train, scheduled hourly departures) rather than real-time arrival countdowns.

findings.claim: The official app does NOT provide live train positions, next-train countdowns, crowding info, or rich accessibility/step-free routing.
findings.detail: Multiple descriptions (official announcement, Uptodown, store search summaries) make no mention of live vehicle positions, real-time delay/countdown, occupancy/crowding, or step-free routing. The official SeferDetaylari page explicitly is a static timetable lookup ('select the hour on your line'), confirming the absence of real-time arrivals in the official digital channel.

findings.claim: User ratings are mediocre-to-poor: ~2.67/5 on iOS and ~3.6/5 reported for Android.
findings.detail: App aggregators report the iOS Metro İstanbul listing at 2.67/5 (small sample, ~12 ratings cited; developer 'METRO ISTANBUL SANAYI VE TICARET ANONIM SIRKETI'). Uptodown reports Android version 3.6 with a 3.6-star rating. Note there appear to be multiple/legacy iOS listings (ids 570644574 and 393533466), and store rating counts are small, so treat exact figures as indicative rather than definitive.

findings.claim: Common complaints: crashes after line additions (e.g. M11), inaccurate arrival estimates, and weak real-time disruption communication.
findings.detail: Review snippets note 'perfect... but crashes after updates' and developers acknowledging the M11 line addition caused crashes. İBB/wider transit complaints on Şikayetvar cite arrival predictions being wrong (e.g. app says bus in 4 min but it doesn't come), QR/payment errors at stations (e.g. Gayrettepe), broken escalators/elevators left uncommunicated, and reduced frequency/overcrowding (M5, M7, M11) with poor info about disruptions. The disruption-communication gap is a recurring theme.

findings.claim: Istanbul's live-train gap is a product gap, not solely a data gap: İBB publishes GTFS and IETT already ships live bus tracking.
findings.detail: İBB Open Data Portal (data.ibb.gov.tr) publishes GTFS static feeds covering Metro, Marmaray (TCDD), IETT bus, ferries, minibus etc. IETT's 'Otobüsüm Nerede' app (replacing Mobiett) already provides live bus locations on a map, arrival ETAs, stop alarms, and route planning across bus/tram/metro/ferry. This shows the institutional capability and data plumbing exist; the official Metro app simply hasn't surfaced live rail positions/ETAs.

findings.claim: Citymapper sets the bar for live trip navigation UX: a 'GO' live trip mode, line-status push alerts, carriage-boarding guidance, and route variants.
findings.detail: Citymapper offers real-time multimodal routing with live departures and bus locations; a 'GO' mode that surfaces all in-trip info; subscribe-to-line push delay alerts; live trip sharing; route variants ('Walk less', 'Simple route' fewest transfers, 'Turbo' fastest); and tells you which carriage (Front/Middle/Back) to board for the fastest exit/interchange. Core features are free.

findings.claim: Transit (app) demonstrates crowdsourced real-time and a 'nearby departures first' home screen.
findings.detail: Transit opens directly to all nearby options (buses, subways, bikeshare) with live departure times; its 'GO' step-by-step navigator turns riders into beacons that broadcast second-by-second vehicle location to others down the line, producing crowdsourced real-time in 175+ cities, often more precise than agency feeds. Gamified with a 'how many people you're helping' nudge and leaderboards.

findings.claim: Moovit offers moving-vehicle live location on map, live step-by-step directions with get-off alerts, real-time crowding, and AR wayfinding.
findings.detail: Moovit shows live vehicle icons moving on the map (buses/trains/trams/subways/ferries) across 220+ cities; Live Directions with 'Get off' alerts; real-time crowding states (Available Seats / Standing Room Only / Crowded) shown across itinerary, nearby, station and line screens; AR Wayfinder to find the stop; plus Moovit+ AI routing extras.

findings.claim: TfL Go is the model for a live status map + first-class accessibility: it greys out closed/disrupted sections and offers a step-free mode with platform-level detail.
findings.detail: TfL Go is built around the live Tube map with live arrivals for Tube/bus/Overground/Elizabeth line/DLR/Tram/National Rail; it greys out map sections closed by engineering works or unplanned disruption; a step-free mode redraws the map to accessible-only stations with platform-level detail (gap width, step height, boarding methods); shows station busyness and facilities (toilets/Wi-Fi); supports TalkBack and dynamic text sizes.

findings.claim: The new MTA app shows real-time train position with platform-boarding markers, per-car crowding, an accessibility map mode, and elevator/escalator status.
findings.detail: NYC's official MTA app provides real-time GPS train/bus positions updated every few seconds with ETA at all future stops; markers showing where to stand on the platform for boarding/exiting; train layout with per-car crowding so riders position before arrival; an accessibility-mode map highlighting accessible stations; easier-to-find elevator/escalator status; and granular service-change alerts for chosen lines/stations.

findings.claim: Trafi and third-party Istanbul apps confirm appetite for a real-time map with moving vehicles and offline support.
findings.detail: Trafi provides a real-time map with moving vehicles, nearby live departures, offline timetables/route search, and saved favorites (home/work/stops) — and historically operated in Turkey. Third-party Istanbul metro apps (e.g. Discover Ukraine 'Istanbul Metro Guide & Planner') win users with accurate offline maps, fast route planner with time/distance, exact trip cost calculator, AR nearest-station, and full offline use — features users praise relative to the official app.

findings.claim: Concrete UX patterns worth adopting for a live Istanbul metro product.
findings.detail: 1) Live network map that animates train positions and greys out disrupted/closed segments (TfL Go + Moovit). 2) Next-train countdowns per station/direction with confidence, plus a 'GO' live-trip mode with get-off alerts (Citymapper/Transit/Moovit). 3) Carriage/platform-position guidance and per-car crowding for fast interchange/exit (Citymapper carriage hints; MTA per-car crowding + platform markers). 4) First-class accessibility: step-free routing mode, real-time elevator/escalator status, platform gap/step detail, TalkBack + dynamic text (TfL Go, MTA). 5) Proactive, line-subscribed disruption push alerts addressing the #1 Istanbul complaint (Citymapper). 6) Open-by-default home screen showing nearby departures first (Transit). 7) Offline maps + trip cost calculator that third-party Istanbul apps already prove demand for.

findings.claim: Pitfalls to avoid, drawn from Metro İstanbul's reviews and best-practice apps.
findings.detail: Avoid: (a) inaccurate ETAs that erode trust (Şikayetvar 'says 4 min, never comes') — show data freshness/confidence and degrade gracefully; (b) brittle releases that crash when a new line is added (M11) — make line config data-driven, not hardcoded; (c) static-only timetables masquerading as 'live' — clearly distinguish scheduled vs real-time; (d) burying disruption info — surface it on the map and via push; (e) shipping fragmented/legacy duplicate store listings (multiple iOS IDs) that split ratings and confuse users; (f) neglecting accessibility (no step-free/elevator status) which best-in-class apps treat as core.

Is there an official Metro İstanbul or İBB developer API (auth/key) exposing live train positions or per-station next-train ETAs, beyond the IETT bus 'Otobüsüm Nerede' system?

dimension: Ideal feature set & prioritized feature catalog for a modern official-grade Istanbul metro app (hero feature: realistic live train-position map)
summary: Istanbul has a usable official data backbone: Metro İstanbul publishes an open, key-free REST API at api.ibb.gov.tr/MetroIstanbul/api/MetroMobile/V2/ (lines, stations, directions, timetables, network maps, ticket prices, multilingual announcements), İBB Open Data adds station/accessibility/transfer datasets, and İBB Şehir Haritası (TUBS) offers multimodal trip planning; the İETT/İBB feed (GetVehicleLocations) carries true real-time bus positions but a comparable per-second metro train GPS feed is NOT publicly documented. The realistic consequence is that the hero "live train map" must be built like Mini Tokyo 3D: schedule/headway-driven interpolation of train positions along line geometry, corrected by whatever low-frequency (~1/min) position or timetable data exists, rather than true GPS dots. The existing official Metro İstanbul app is weak (App Store 2.4/5, complaints about no exit guidance and stale equipment status), so a polished live-map-first app is a clear differentiation opportunity. The catalog below maps every user-listed feature plus high-value additions from Transit, Citymapper, Moovit and TfL Go into MoSCoW tiers anchored on the live-position experience. Key scoping constraint: İstanbulkart in Apple Wallet / iPhone Express Transit is not supported in Turkey, so deep-link to the official İstanbulkart app rather than building native top-up.

findings.claim: Metro İstanbul exposes a public, key-free REST API (MetroMobile V2) that is the primary data source for stations, lines, timetables, maps, prices, and announcements.
findings.detail: Confirmed endpoints on the official help/api host include GET api.ibb.gov.tr/MetroIstanbul/api/MetroMobile/V2/GetLines, /GetMaps (network map list), /GetTimeTable (trip timetables), /GetTicketPrice/{lang} (fare list), and /GetAnnouncements/{Language} (multilingual service announcements). The İBB Open Data portal mirrors these as 'web services' (station info list, rail-system group list, direction-by-line, direction-by-line+station, line info list, FAQ) under the İBB Open Data License. Data appears free with no documented API key and 'no specific rate limits.' This backbone directly powers: line list, station list, first/last train, frequency, line status, announcements/maintenance, ticket prices, and multi-language.

findings.claim: A true per-second GPS feed of metro train positions is NOT publicly documented; the realistic live-map hero feature must be interpolated from schedules/headways like Mini Tokyo 3D.
findings.detail: İBB/İETT real-time vehicle location (GetVehicleLocations, polled ~once per minute via VehicleLocationCacheService) covers BUSES with speed and plate. No equivalent documented real-time metro train-position endpoint surfaced; the metro API is schedule/timetable-centric. The proven pattern for a 'realistic' moving-train map without GPS is schedule-based interpolation: place trains along line geometry between stations using scheduled/known travel times and current time, then snap/correct when any low-frequency position or arrival data is available. Mini Tokyo 3D (open source, MIT) does exactly this from GTFS / GTFS-RT, animating thousands of trains smoothly with time-based playback. This means the hero feature is achievable today but should be honestly framed as a high-fidelity simulation, with confidence/'estimated' indicators.

findings.claim: GTFS static data for Istanbul rail (Metro + Marmaray/TCDD) is available and is the cleanest input for the interpolation engine and for trip planning.
findings.detail: İBB publishes GTFS-building data for Istanbul transit including Metro and Marmaray (TCDD), and IETT GTFS is on data.ibb.gov.tr and ulasav.csb.gov.tr. GTFS gives stop coordinates, route shapes, and stop_times — exactly the ingredients to draw line geometry and interpolate train positions, and to compute journey times/transfers offline. Marmaray runs ~15 min headways Gebze–Halkalı (8 min on the core Zeytinburnu–Maltepe section) with weekend night service to ~03:00; first trains ~06:00 — useful for headway-based simulation when stop_times are coarse.

findings.claim: Top transit apps converge on a set of high-value features beyond the user's list: one-tap nearby real-time departures, GO trip companion with Live Activities/Dynamic Island, line-subscription alerts, offline maps, and 'best car / which exit' guidance.
findings.detail: Transit (rated best-designed iOS transit app) opens straight to nearby lines with live wait times, offers a 'GO' navigation companion that ticks down remaining stops in Live Activities and auto-expands the Dynamic Island ~2 stops before alighting, shows which platform/exit and which end of the train to board, and has Apple Watch complications. Citymapper lets users subscribe to favorite lines for automatic disruption/status alerts, shows which subway car to board, offline maps, and live boarding info. These map onto the user's 'approaching trains', 'next train', 'transfer info', 'which exit' and 'notifications' items and raise the bar for them.

findings.claim: Real-time crowding/occupancy is a marquee feature (Moovit, TfL Go) and can be done as a credible simulation when no sensor feed exists.
findings.detail: Moovit surfaces crowdedness as Available Seats / Standing Room Only / Crowded across itinerary, live-directions, station, line-detail and favorites screens, plus wheelchair accessibility per line. TfL Go shows 'how busy a station is right now.' Where Istanbul lacks a sensor feed, research supports estimating occupancy heuristically from headway, time-of-day demand profiles and historical patterns — matching the user's 'simulated line crowding' goal. It must be clearly labeled as estimated to retain 'official-grade' trust.

findings.claim: Accessibility and station-detail depth (step-free mode, exits, facilities, equipment status) are differentiators, and are exactly where the current official Metro İstanbul app fails.
findings.detail: TfL Go offers a step-free mode that redraws the map to accessible-only stations, shows platform gap width / step height / boarding method, station busyness, toilets and Wi-Fi, and supports VoiceOver + Dynamic Type. The official Metro İstanbul app (App Store 2.4/5, 93 reviews) is criticized for NOT marking precise exits and for showing non-functional equipment without maintenance info — directly validating the user's 'which exit', 'elevator/accessibility', 'station facilities (WC/parking/bike)' and 'out-of-service alerts' features as high-value gaps to win on. İBB/Metro datasets include station escalators/elevators, disabled charging points, restrooms/prayer rooms and transfer info to feed these.

findings.claim: Station entrance/exit and 'best car to board' guidance can be sourced from OpenStreetMap when official indoor data is missing.
findings.detail: OSM tags railway=subway_entrance for entrances/exits and supports indoor station mapping (OpenStationMap), plus a destination:carriages tag to define which exit is nearest each train carriage — the data substrate for 'which exit to use' and 'best car to board'. Apps like Exit Strategy NYC, Citymapper NYC and the NY Subway MTA Map app already ship best-car guidance. For Istanbul this is a Could/later-tier enrichment layer over the core map.

findings.claim: MUST (MVP) tier — the live-position hero plus the table-stakes that make it useful.
findings.detail: 1) Realistic live train-position map (schedule/headway interpolation along GTFS/line geometry, trains animate between stations, per-direction, with 'estimated' confidence styling) — the hero. 2) Line list + per-line live status (running/disrupted/closed) via GetLines + GetAnnouncements. 3) Next-train / approaching-trains per station + first/last train + frequency (timetable-driven). 4) # of trains currently running per line (derived from the simulation). 5) Station detail: location, transfers, exits, accessibility (elevator/escalator status), facilities (WC/parking/bike). 6) Nearest station ('near me'). 7) Quick map search + advanced filtering (by line/mode/accessibility). 8) Favorites (stations + lines) and recent searches. 9) Out-of-service / maintenance alerts and announcements (multilingual). 10) Multi-language (TR/EN at minimum, from GetAnnouncements/{lang} + GetTicketPrice/{lang}). 11) Dark/light theme. 12) Journey-time calc + transfer info between two stations (offline from GTFS). Rationale: this set delivers the hero feature with the surrounding context users need on day one and covers nearly all of the user's explicit list.

findings.claim: SHOULD tier — strong value, build right after MVP.
findings.detail: 1) GO-style trip companion: pick a journey and get a Live Activity / Dynamic Island countdown of remaining stops + 'get off next' alert (Transit's signature). 2) Line-subscription push notifications for disruptions on favorite lines/stations (Citymapper pattern). 3) Simulated line/car crowding indicator (Available/Standing/Crowded) labeled 'estimated' from headway+time-of-day. 4) Full A-to-B multimodal trip planning incl. Marmaray/tram/metrobüs (leverage İBB TUBS or local GTFS routing). 5) Offline maps + cached schedules (works underground with no signal). 6) Home-screen widgets + Apple Watch / Wear complications for nearest-departures. 7) 'Which exit to use' and 'best car to board' (OSM-sourced). 8) Share live ETA / location of an in-progress trip. Rationale: these are the features that elevate the app from informative to indispensable and match what Transit/Citymapper/Moovit win on.

findings.claim: COULD tier — nice-to-have polish and breadth.
findings.detail: 1) 3D map mode (Mini Tokyo 3D-style) and time-machine playback (scrub to any time to see scheduled operations). 2) Fare/İstanbulkart info screen + transfer-discount calculator, deep-linking to the official İstanbulkart app for top-up (native Wallet is blocked in TR). 3) Indoor station maps / platform-level wayfinding from OSM. 4) Crowd-sourced confirmations (delay/crowding reports) to refine estimates. 5) Service-bulletin archive & planned-works calendar. 6) Personalized commute card / 'your usual trip' surfaced at the right time of day. 7) CarPlay/Android Auto nearby-departures. Rationale: differentiators that deepen engagement once the core is solid.

findings.claim: WON'T (now) tier — explicitly out of scope for v1 due to platform/data constraints.
findings.detail: 1) Native İstanbulkart top-up / Apple Wallet Express Transit — Apple Pay/Wallet transit cards are not supported in Turkey and iPhone NFC top-up is blocked, so this cannot be delivered natively (deep-link only). 2) True GPS per-train tracking — no public real-time metro position feed is documented; promising it would be dishonest, hence the interpolation approach instead. 3) In-app ticket purchase / payment. Rationale: setting these aside keeps v1 honest and shippable; revisit if/when Apple Wallet launches in TR or Metro İstanbul publishes a GTFS-RT VehiclePositions feed for rail.

dataSources.notes: First/last/headway reference for Marmaray (~06:00 first; 15-min Gebze–Halkalı, 8-min core; weekend nights to ~03:00). Use to validate/seed headway-based simulation if GTFS stop_times are coarse.

dataSources.name: Official Metro İstanbul app (competitive baseline)
dataSources.notes: Rated 2.4/5; complaints: no precise exit marking, stale/non-functional equipment status, rough UI. Defines the bar to beat and validates differentiators (live map, exit guidance, reliable accessibility status).

openQuestions: Does Metro İstanbul expose ANY low-frequency real-time train position or live arrival/countdown endpoint (e.g. a station-display feed) that could correct the interpolation? Not found publicly — needs direct testing of the api.ibb.gov.tr/MetroIstanbul/Help endpoint list (some pages 404'd in research).
Is there an official GTFS-RT (TripUpdates / VehiclePositions) feed for Istanbul rail specifically, or only GTFS-static? Only static GTFS and bus realtime were confirmed.

What are the actual response schemas and field names for GetTimeTable, GetLines, station and direction services (needed to design the interpolation and next-train logic)? Help pages did not render full field lists via fetch.

Does the unofficial TypeScript wrapper (search-cached as 16-96/ or ardytstrn/metro.istanbul) still exist / what endpoints does it document? Repo returned 404 on direct API and raw fetches — verify current slug before relying on it.
TUBS trip-planning API: what are the exact key-issuance process, rate limits, and cost? Pricing undisclosed.
Are İBB/Metro web services served with CORS for direct browser calls, or is a backend proxy mandatory (PublicAPI says CORS not supported for data.ibb.gov.tr)?
Licensing fine print of the İBB Open Data License for a production consumer app (attribution, commercial use, caching/redistribution of derived live-map data).
Granularity/reliability of station escalator/elevator status data — is it live or static? The official app's stale-equipment complaint suggests it may not be real-time.

dimension: Design System Foundations for an Official-Grade 2026 Istanbul Metro App (brand identity, line color palette, transit map visualization, 2026 UI standards, map rendering tech, and underlying live-data sources)
summary: Istanbul's rail network has a well-defined, publicly verifiable per-line color palette (sourced from Wikipedia's machine-readable Adjacent stations module, which mirrors official signage), but Metro İstanbul AŞ does not publish a fully open corporate brand-identity manual. The governing brand is İstanbul Büyükşehir Belediyesi (İBB), whose public corporate-identity guide specifies Mark Pro as the official typeface; the Metro İstanbul logo is a dark-blue (cobalt, ~#2E3192) roundel with a red M. For data, the only official open feed (data.ibb.gov.tr) is static GTFS covering metro, Marmaray, ferry and bus, but it is frozen (2021-2023, explicitly "will not be updated"); the practical live source for moving-vehicle dots is the legacy İBB/İETT SOAP API (api.ibb.gov.tr) which returns live bus GPS but not native metro/Marmaray train positions, so train motion must be simulated from schedule/headway data. For 2026 UI, the authoritative standards are Material 3 / Material 3 Expressive (tonal elevation, dynamic color, spring-based motion) and Apple HIG with the new Liquid Glass material, both with explicit reduced-motion/contrast accessibility hooks aligned to WCAG 2.2. For rendering many moving markers, MapLibre GL JS (free, open, BSD, no token) is the recommended engine, animating vehicles as a GeoJSON source + circle/symbol layer on the GPU rather than DOM markers.

findings.claim: The Istanbul rail network's official per-line colors are publicly available as exact hex codes via Wikipedia's machine-readable Adjacent stations module.
findings.detail: Verified hex values: M1/M1A/M1B #EE2229 (red); M2 #059A4D (green); M3 #0CA6DF (light blue/cyan); M4 #E81E77 (pink/magenta); M5 #683166 (purple); M6 #C9AA79 (tan/gold); M7 #F490B3 (pink); M8 #487ABF (blue); M9 #FCD10D (yellow); M10 #4CAA3C (green); M11 #A1609B (purple); M12 #CAD300 (lime); M13 #FF4B58; M14 #B16400. Trams: T1 #004B86, T2 #90ABA0, T3 #99562F, T4 #FF7E42, T5 #7B72B2, T6 #E77C7C. Funicular F1/F3/F4 #7A745A. Marmaray #5A5F5C (gray). These match the colors used on the official Metro İstanbul network map/signage and should be the canonical line palette for the app.

findings.claim: The official corporate typeface of İstanbul Büyükşehir Belediyesi (İBB), the parent brand of Metro İstanbul, is Mark Pro.
findings.detail: The İBB Corporate Identity Guide (Kurumsal Kimlik Kılavuzu, dated 09.06.2020, PDF hosted on uploads.ibb.istanbul) lists the Mark Pro family (Bold, Black, Medium, Light, Book) as the official typeface. Mark Pro is a commercial font (FontFont/Monotype). For a web app, a close, freely-licensed substitute would be a geometric humanist sans such as Inter, Mona Sans, or Plus Jakarta Sans. The guide is created in Adobe Illustrator and uses CMYK separations; exact corporate hex/Pantone values were not text-extractable from the PDF in this environment.

findings.claim: The Metro İstanbul logo is a dark-blue roundel with a red M; the dominant brand color is a cobalt/navy blue.
findings.detail: The Metro İstanbul wordmark/roundel logo uses a dark blue circle background with red, white and dark blue as the core palette. Third-party logo color analysis (whatthelogo.com) extracted the palette as: cherry red #ED1C24, cobalt #2E3192, faded blue #6F70B4, blush #F19997, cloudy blue #B2B1D6. The official Metro İstanbul website (metro.istanbul) is built around a blue/navy primary with the same red accent. NOTE: these specific hex values are reverse-engineered from logo imagery, not from an official Metro İstanbul brand book (which does not appear to be public), so treat the red/blue as directionally official but confirm exact values against vector logo assets before production. Vector logo assets are downloadable from Wikimedia Commons and seeklogo.

findings.claim: Metro İstanbul AŞ does not publish a fully open, public corporate brand/visual-identity manual; brand governance flows from İBB.
findings.detail: Searches in Turkish and English for a Metro İstanbul kurumsal kimlik kılavuzu returned only the İBB municipal corporate guide and the İETT/metrobus guide, plus academic/commentary articles noting the logo (designed by Faruk Çağla, 1992) was revised around 2016 when the operator renamed to 'Metro İstanbul'. The official metro.istanbul site footer contains no developer/brand-asset/open-data section. Logo usage permissions are handled via a manual 'Kurumsal İzinler' request form, implying brand assets are gated rather than openly licensed.

findings.claim: Material Design 3 / Material 3 Expressive is the authoritative Android/cross-platform 2026 UI standard: tonal elevation, dynamic color, and a new spring-based motion system.
findings.detail: MD3 expresses elevation primarily via tonal surface color overlays (a semi-transparent primary tint) plus shadows. Elevation level tokens map to dp: Level 0 = 0dp (flat: filled buttons, outlined cards), Level 1 = 1dp (elevated cards, bottom sheets), Level 2 = 3dp (navigation bar, menus), Level 3 = 6dp (FAB, dialogs), Level 4 = 8dp and Level 5 = 12dp (mostly hover states). Dynamic color derives a full tonal palette (13 tones, 0=black to 100=white) for Primary/Secondary/Tertiary/Neutral/Error from one source color and is accessible-by-default. Material 3 Expressive (2025) replaces fixed easing/duration with a spring-based 'motion physics' system, adds 35 new shapes with morph capability, new components (button groups, FAB menus, split buttons, loading indicators), and is backed by 46 studies / 18,000+ participants.

findings.claim: Apple Human Interface Guidelines (2025/iOS 26) introduce Liquid Glass, a translucent adaptive material, with built-in accessibility modifiers.
findings.detail: Liquid Glass is a dynamic, translucent material unifying Apple platforms; it renders in real time with specular highlights, bends/concentrates light, and lets content show through navigation/controls. It has two variants: 'regular' (blurs + adjusts luminosity for legibility) and 'clear'. Accessibility modifiers: Reduced Transparency (frostier, obscures background), Increased Contrast (predominantly black/white with contrasting borders), and Reduced Motion (lowers effect intensity). HIG guidance: every animation should have a purpose (confirm action, reinforce hierarchy, guide attention). Relevant for a glass-style overlay HUD layer above the live map.

findings.claim: Motion must be purposeful and reduced-motion-aware to meet WCAG 2.2; UI animations should sit in the ~200-500ms range.
findings.detail: Best-practice principles: only necessary motion; short and subtle; don't move without user intent; provide stop/reduce controls; respect prefers-reduced-motion. Typical UI durations 200-500ms, baseline 200-300ms; ease-in-out for general UI, deceleration curves for emphasis. WCAG 2.3.3 (AAA) lets interaction-triggered motion be disabled unless essential; WCAG 2.2.2 (Pause, Stop, Hide) requires controls for continuously moving/auto-updating content. For a map with continuously moving train dots this means: provide a way to pause animation, honor prefers-reduced-motion (e.g. fade/jump position updates instead of tweening), and keep motion subtle.

findings.claim: MapLibre GL JS is the recommended web map engine for animating many moving vehicles; render vehicles as a GeoJSON source + circle/symbol layer on the GPU, not as DOM markers.
findings.detail: MapLibre GL JS is a free, open-source (BSD) fork of Mapbox GL JS v1 with no access token / no usage billing, near-identical API (easy migration), and GPU vector rendering. Both MapLibre and Mapbox render on the GPU; adding ~100k points to a GeoJSON source still renders each frame under 16ms (60fps). A 2025 academic benchmark found Mapbox GL JS marginally fastest at 50k+ points, then OpenLayers, MapLibre, Leaflet; GL libraries have slower init but flat scaling. Recommended pattern for moving trains: keep all vehicles in one GeoJSON source, update coordinates with setData() on each tick / requestAnimationFrame, render via a circle or symbol layer (line-colored dots). Avoid per-vehicle DOM Marker objects which don't scale. Leaflet is simpler but raster/DOM-based and weaker for many animated markers; Mapbox GL JS is feature-rich but requires a token and is commercially licensed (v2+ proprietary).

findings.claim: Transit map design favors octolinear (45/90-degree) schematic layouts for the network diagram, while live vehicle tracking benefits from geographic accuracy.
findings.detail: Schematic transit maps (Beck/London Underground style) use octolinear layouts (horizontal 0deg, vertical 90deg, diagonals 45/135deg) which usability studies rate as significantly more usable/preferred than curvilinear; they prioritize topological clarity over geographic accuracy. Key legibility rules: simple line trajectories, careful label placement, and avoid distorting relative N-S/E-W ordering of station pairs. Practical implication for this app: a geographically-accurate MapLibre basemap is correct for plotting real GPS-style moving train dots, but an optional octolinear schematic overlay/mode improves at-a-glance route comprehension. Combining both (geographic for position truth, schematic for wayfinding) is the strongest pattern.

findings.claim: Dashboard/data-viz panels (next-train countdowns, headways, KPIs) should follow a restrained semantic-color, high-contrast, hierarchy-first pattern.
findings.detail: Best practices: place primary KPIs top/left with larger type; limit a view to <=5 decision-driving metrics; use a three-color semantic palette (green=good, red=bad, gray=neutral) rather than rainbow gradients; avoid red/green-only encoding (colorblind risk) and add non-color cues (icons, labels, patterns); ensure sufficient contrast and screen-reader support; show trend/benchmark context next to numbers. For a transit app this maps to countdown cards, on-time/delay status chips, and headway/occupancy indicators with semantic colors layered on top of the line-color identity.

findings.claim: The only official open transit dataset for Istanbul (data.ibb.gov.tr GTFS) is static and frozen, not real-time.
findings.detail: data.ibb.gov.tr publishes 'Toplu Ulaşım GTFS Verisi' (standard GTFS CSVs: agency, calendar, frequencies, routes, shapes, stop_times, stops, trips) covering Metro, Marmaray (TCDD), Şehir Hatları/İDO/Turyol/Dentur (ferry), minibüs/dolmuş, plus a separate İETT bus GTFS feed. License: İBB Açık Veri Lisansı (free). Critical limitation: the dataset is explicitly marked 'Bu veri güncellenmeyecektir' (will not be updated), with last updates 2021-2023. Free download; the portal asks for a free 'İBB Hesabım' account but no paid API key. This is usable for static routes/stops/schedules but cannot drive live train positions.

findings.claim: No official GTFS-Realtime feed for Istanbul metro/Marmaray train positions was found; the practical live source is the legacy İBB/İETT SOAP API, which exposes live bus GPS only.
findings.detail: Searches (TR + EN), Transitland, and Mobility Database did not surface an official İBB/Metro İstanbul/TCDD GTFS-RT (VehiclePositions/TripUpdates) endpoint. The functioning live source is the legacy SOAP API at base https://api.ibb.gov.tr/iett with services: /UlasimAnaVeri/HatDurakGuzergah.asmx?wsdl (GetDurak_json - stops) and /FiloDurum/SeferGerceklesme.asmx?wsdl (GetHatOtoKonum_json - live positions per route; GetFiloAracKonum_json - all vehicles). It returns live GPS (lat/long, speed, plate, KapiNo, nearest stop, timestamp) for BUSES; community reports note it is slow and historically went offline nightly after ~00:15. Implication: for a metro/Marmaray app, moving train dots must be SIMULATED by interpolating along GTFS shapes using scheduled times/headways (Marmaray: ~8 min Pendik-Atakoy, ~15 min Halkali-Gebze; service ~05:58-23:28 weekdays, to ~01:28 Fri/Sat), optionally validated against station next-train countdowns.

Is there ANY official real-time train-position or next-train-arrival API for Metro İstanbul or Marmaray? None found publicly. Station displays and the Metro İstanbul app clearly consume something internal - worth a direct inquiry to Metro İstanbul / İBB Bilgi İşlem and inspecting the official app's network traffic.
Current reliability/availability of the legacy api.ibb.gov.tr SOAP service in 2026 (it historically went offline nightly and is slow) - needs live testing before depending on it for the bus layer.
