dimension: Schedule-based vehicle-position simulation: methods, projects, algorithms, and a recommended architecture for a live-feeling Istanbul metro map without real GPS
summary: When no live GPS feed exists, you synthesize "ghost" vehicles by computing, for the current wall-clock time T, which trips are in motion and where each one is along its route shape. The standard pipeline is: (1) expand the GTFS schedule into concrete trip instances (directly from stop_times.txt, or by expanding frequencies.txt via trip_start = start_time + x*headway_secs); (2) for each active trip, find the stop-pair bracketing T and linearly interpolate a fraction f = (T - dep_prev)/(arr_next - dep_prev); (3) convert that fraction into a distance-along-shape using shape_dist_traveled (or cumulative segment length) and map it to a lat/lng on the polyline with a turf.along-style lookup; (4) tick every frame and ease/snap dots so they glide smoothly. This is the exact approach used by real projects: vasile/transit-map (the engine behind swisstrains.ch), the Mapbox "animate point along route" example, and the Transitland/TransitFlow Processing animations. Crucially for this app, the official İBB "Toplu Ulaşım GTFS" dataset already ships stops, trips, stop_times, shapes AND frequencies for Metro İstanbul and Marmaray, but has NO official real-time feed (it is explicitly frozen), which is precisely why a schedule-driven simulation engine is the correct architecture. The hard parts -- shared track, turnarounds, and bidirectional flow -- are handled cleanly by treating each direction_id as its own set of trips on its own shape rather than trying to model physical track sharing. Recommended: a lightweight precompute step plus a client-side requestAnimationFrame ticker using Turf.js (length/along/bearing), with lerp easing between frames and nearest-point-on-line snapping to keep dots glued to the polyline.

findings.claim: Two GTFS schedule representations drive interpolation: explicit stop_times.txt (absolute arrival/departure per stop) and compressed frequencies.txt (headway-based). When a trip appears in frequencies.txt, the absolute times in stop_times.txt are ignored and only used as RELATIVE inter-stop travel times, re-anchored to each generated start time.
findings.detail: stop_times.txt gives fixed arrival_time/departure_time per (trip, stop_sequence). frequencies.txt gives start_time, end_time, headway_secs and exact_times for a representative trip; you generate real trips and reuse the stop_times offsets. exact_times=1 means evenly scheduled trips; exact_times=0/empty means the operator merely targets the headway (no fixed schedule). For a metro this is ideal because it is compact and matches how metros actually run (every N minutes).

findings.claim: The exact frequencies.txt trip-expansion formula is trip_start_time = start_time + x * headway_secs for x = 0,1,2,... while trip_start_time < end_time. Each generated trip inherits the relative stop offsets, so stop k departs at trip_start_time + (departure_offset_k - departure_offset_0).
findings.detail: This is the canonical algorithm to turn one frequency row into N concrete vehicles. For a metro line running 06:00-24:00 at 300s headway you generate ~216 trips per direction per period; at time T only the handful whose [first_dep, last_arr] window contains T are 'in motion'. This is the cheap, deterministic core of the simulation -- no DB or streaming required.

findings.claim: Position-at-time-T is two nested linear interpolations: first in TIME between the two bracketing stops to get a fraction f, then in SPACE along the route shape using shape_dist_traveled (or cumulative polyline length) to convert f into a lat/lng.
findings.detail: Step 1 (time->fraction): find stops i and i+1 with dep_i <= T < arr_{i+1}; f = (T - dep_i) / (arr_{i+1} - dep_i), clamped to [0,1]; during dwell (dep_i within an arrival window) f=0 so the dot pauses at the platform. Step 2 (fraction->distance): target_dist = shape_dist_traveled_i + f * (shape_dist_traveled_{i+1} - shape_dist_traveled_i). Step 3 (distance->point): walk the shape segments until cumulative length >= target_dist, then interpolate within that segment. If shape_dist_traveled is absent in stop_times, you first snap each stop to the shape (nearest-point-on-line) to get its distance, then proceed identically.

findings.claim: vasile/transit-map is the most directly relevant open-source reference: it animates vehicle markers from public-transport timetables by interpolating positions along route polylines, and it is the actual engine behind production simulations like swisstrains.ch.
findings.detail: Architecture is a clean server/client split. Server exposes time-parameterized APIs -- api/getTrips/[hhmm] returns vehicles active at a given time and api/getDepartures/[stop_id]/[hhmm] returns station departures -- and serves route geometry + stops as GeoJSON FeatureCollections (generated from GTFS via a companion 'GTFS-viz'/gtfs-to-geojson step). Client polls for the current simulation time and interpolates between consecutive stops by elapsed time. A time_multiply parameter (1,5,10,100) accelerates the clock and an hms override sets time-of-day -- both very useful patterns for a demo app. This is essentially the blueprint for the Istanbul app.

findings.claim: The canonical CLIENT-side rendering technique is Mapbox GL JS + Turf.js: measure the line with turf.length, pre-sample or query points with turf.along(line, distanceKm), and compute heading with turf.bearing between consecutive points, advancing a counter each requestAnimationFrame.
findings.detail: Mapbox's official 'Animate a point along a route' example pre-samples the line into N steps (e.g. 500) via a loop i += lineDistance/steps calling turf.along, stores the coordinate array, then on each animation frame sets the marker to coords[counter] and bearing = turf.bearing(prev,next). The vanilla example uses uniform stepping with NO easing; for schedule-driven motion you instead drive the distance from the time-interpolation above so speed reflects the real timetable (slower near stations, paused during dwell).

findings.claim: Multiple independent open-source projects validate the schedule->animation approach: Transitland/TransitFlow (Processing + Unfolding Maps, linear interpolation between scheduled stops), the Monday Maps PostGIS pipeline, and several 'animate-gtfs' repos.
findings.detail: TransitFlow downloads schedules, then 'plots trips using linear interpolation' between consecutive scheduled stops, stepping through a day. The Monday Maps tutorial does the same in PostGIS: convert times to minutes-since-midnight, build lines between sequential stops, subdivide with ST_LineSubstring, generate points every 15s (0.25 min) with generate_series, and render with Carto Torque keyed on departure_time. The recurring pattern across all of them: minutes-since-midnight as the time axis + linear interpolation along the shape.

findings.claim: Shared track, turnarounds and bidirectional flow are best handled at the SCHEDULE level (one trip set per direction_id, each bound to its own directional shape), not by physically modeling track occupancy.
findings.detail: In GTFS each direction is a separate trip with direction_id 0/1 and typically its own shape (often the reverse geometry of the other). You simulate outbound and inbound trips completely independently -- two dots can occupy 'the same' corridor because they ride two superposed but distinct polylines, which is exactly how SUMO models bidirectional rail (two edges with reversed geometry, spreadType=center). Turnarounds at terminals are implicit: when a trip's last stop time passes, that vehicle simply disappears; the next departure in the opposite direction is a different generated trip. You do NOT need block/signal logic for a visual app. If you want continuity (a train visibly turning back), link an inbound trip to the outbound that just terminated via block_id. For realism, terminals need two 'pockets' (TCRP) but that is beyond a visualizer's needs.

findings.claim: To 'feel real', snap dots exactly onto the line geometry and ease between frames; transit best-practice even mandates positions stay within 200 m of shapes.txt, and real feeds refresh <=30s with data no older than 90s -- useful targets for your simulated cadence.
findings.detail: Snapping: use turf.nearestPointOnLine to project any computed/jittered point back onto the polyline; its returned 'location' (distance along line) and 'index' let you keep the dot glued to the track and even derive bearing. Easing: because timetable speed is piecewise-constant between stops, interpolate the distance-along-line each frame (lerp toward target) so acceleration/deceleration looks smooth; pause f=0 during dwell. Jitter: add small bounded noise to headway/dwell per trip so trains don't move in lockstep (mimics real bunching) -- mesoscopic transit models inject exactly this stochasticity (dispatch, run-time, dwell) and reproduce realistic bunching. Cadence: you control the clock, so tick at 60fps for motion but recompute the active-trip set at a coarser interval (e.g. every 1-5s), echoing the 30s/90s real-feed norms.

findings.claim: Recommended architecture for THIS app: a lightweight precompute (GTFS -> per-line directional polyline + ordered stops with cumulative distance + frequency table) shipped as JSON/GeoJSON, plus a pure client-side requestAnimationFrame ticker that computes positions on the fly. No backend or live feed needed.
findings.detail: Pipeline: (a) Build step (Node, using gtfs-to-geojson or a small parser): for each route+direction emit a GeoJSON LineString (the shape) and an array of stops with shape_dist_traveled; flatten frequencies.txt into {start,end,headway,stop_offsets[]}. (b) Runtime (browser): every frame get now (or accelerated sim time); for each frequency window generate active trip start times (the start+x*headway formula); for each active trip compute f via the time-interpolation, target_dist via shape_dist_traveled, point via turf.along / manual segment walk, bearing via turf.bearing; lerp from last frame and render. (c) This is exactly the transit-map model but fully client-side -- O(active_trips) per frame, trivially handles all ~20 Istanbul metro/Marmaray lines. Optional thin backend only if you later want to swap in a true GTFS-RT feed; until then the simulation IS the source of truth.

findings.claim: Critical data reality for Istanbul: the official İBB GTFS dataset includes shapes.txt, stop_times.txt AND frequencies.txt for Metro İstanbul and Marmaray, but there is NO official public real-time/GPS feed -- the static dataset is explicitly frozen.
findings.detail: data.ibb.gov.tr 'Toplu Ulaşım GTFS Verisi' covers Metro Istanbul, Marmaray (TCDD), Minibus, Taksi Dolmus, Sehir Hatlari, Turyol, IDO, Dentur Avrasya; files: agency, calendar, routes, stops, trips, stop_times, shapes, frequencies (CSV), under the IBB Open Data License, free. It carries the note 'Bu veri guncellenmeyecektir' (this data will not be updated) and was last refreshed 2021-2023. No GTFS-RT vehicle-positions endpoint is published. This confirms a schedule-based simulation is not just convenient but NECESSARY -- there is no live GPS to consume, so synthesizing ghost vehicles from the timetable is the only path to a live-feeling map. (Caveat: the static feed may be stale vs current service patterns; validate first/last departures and headways against metro.istanbul published schedules.)

openQuestions: Is the frozen İBB GTFS dump's frequency/headway and first/last-departure data still accurate for 2026 service? It must be cross-checked against metro.istanbul published schedules, and recent line extensions (e.g. new M-line stations) may be missing entirely.

Does the İBB GTFS include shape_dist_traveled values in stop_times.txt, or only geometry in shapes.txt? If absent, the build step must snap each stop to its shape (turf.nearestPointOnLine) to derive per-stop distances -- needs to be verified by inspecting the actual files.

Are inbound/outbound directions stored as two separate shapes (reverse geometry) or a single bidirectional shape? This determines whether you can treat directions fully independently or must offset the two dots laterally to avoid overlap.

Do any UNOFFICIAL real-time sources exist for Istanbul rail (e.g. third-party apps, Moovit/Trafi back-ends, a hidden metro.istanbul endpoint)? None were found in official channels; worth a deeper scrape/network-inspection if true live positions are ever desired.

How should service variations (weekday/weekend/holiday via calendar.txt, and reduced late-night frequencies) be selected at runtime so the simulated count of trains matches reality at any given time/date?

What dwell-time assumption to use when stop_times encodes equal arrival=departure (no explicit dwell)? A small constant dwell (e.g. 20-30s) plus jitter is likely needed to make platform pauses look real.

dimension: Istanbul open-data transit datasets (data.ibb.gov.tr, IETT, Metro İstanbul, TCDD/Marmaray) — what is exposed RIGHT NOW
summary: Istanbul's primary open-data hub is the İBB Açık Veri Portalı (data.ibb.gov.tr), a CKAN portal with a public read API (datastore_search / datastore_search_sql, no key needed for reads) and ~179 datasets in the mobility category. For rail it exposes: (1) a comprehensive multimodal GTFS feed ("Toplu Ulaşım GTFS Verisi") covering Metro + Marmaray + ferries + minibus as 8 CSV files — but this is FROZEN/STALE (Metro data from 2023-01-09, Marmaray from 2022-05-22; the page explicitly says "this data will not be updated"); (2) a separate İETT GTFS feed (bus + nostalgic tram) that IS actively maintained (last modified 2026-04-21); (3) GeoJSON vector datasets for rail lines and station points (updated 2025-06-05); and (4) a large set of Metro İstanbul REST web services under api.ibb.gov.tr/MetroIstanbul (GetLines, GetStations, GetTimeTable, GetServiceStatus, etc.) — these are static reference data (lines, stations, timetables, service status), NOT real-time train positions. The only real-time transit feed is İETT's SOAP service (SeferGerceklesme.asmx) for live BUS positions/trip realization — there is NO real-time train/metro position API. TCDD/Marmaray publishes no official GTFS or public API of its own; its schedules live only on tcddtasimacilik.gov.tr webpages, and the only Marmaray GTFS that exists is the stale İBB bundle. Access is free under the İBB Open Data License; SOAP services ship with usage-doc PDFs.

findings.claim: data.ibb.gov.tr publishes a comprehensive multimodal GTFS feed that includes Metro and Marmaray, but it is frozen and explicitly will not be updated.
findings.detail: Dataset 'Toplu Ulaşım GTFS Verisi' / 'Public Transport GTFS Data' (slug public-transport-gtfs-data, package id 121a9892-7945-419a-9b89-49f6083926df), published by Bilgi İşlem Dairesi Başkanlığı (Dept. of IT). 8 CSV resources: agency, calendar, frequencies, routes, shapes, stop_times, stops, trips (direct download URLs like https://data.ibb.gov.tr/dataset/121a9892-.../resource/d1f7c258-bbc1-406f-9ab2-7a7c1797c673/download/stops.csv). Covers minibus, taksi dolmuş, Şehir Hatları, Turyol, Dentur Avrasya, İDO, Marmaray (TCDD), Metro. CKAN metadata_modified = 2025-03-12. CRITICAL: per-operator currency is old (Metro 2023-01-09, Marmaray 2022-05-22, minibüs 2022-11-08, etc.) and the page states the data will not be updated. So for a live metro app this is reference geometry/schedule only, already 3+ years stale for rail.

findings.claim: The İETT GTFS feed is a separate dataset and IS actively maintained (last updated April 2026).
findings.detail: Dataset 'İETT GTFS Verisi' / 'IETT GTFS Data' (slug iett-gtfs-verisi, package id 8540e256-6df5-4719-85bc-e64e91508ede), published by İETT. Resources: agency, calendar, routes, stops, trips, stop_times (all CSV) plus a stop_times.zip. CKAN metadata_modified = 2026-04-21T12:52:23. Created 2022-02-23. This is bus-focused (İETT operates buses + the nostalgic trams T2 Taksim-Tünel / T5 historic), so its rail relevance is limited to nostalgic tram lines, but it is the one GTFS on the portal that is current.

findings.claim: Metro İstanbul exposes a full suite of static REST web services under api.ibb.gov.tr/MetroIstanbul/api/MetroMobile/V2/, but none return real-time train positions.
findings.detail: Confirmed live endpoints (each a separate CKAN dataset): GetLines (hat bilgisi), GetStations (istasyon bilgi listesi — metadata_modified 2026-02-27, the freshest), GetStationById/{LineId}, GetDirections (yön), GetTimeTable (sefer tarifeleri), GetServiceStatus (hat hizmet durumu — closest thing to 'live', returns line up/down status), GetMaps (ağ haritası), GetTicketPrice/TR, GetAddresses/{Language}, GetRailwayGroups, GetActivities, GetLineProjects, GetFailureTypes, FrequentlyAskedQuestions, GetTechnicalObjectTypes. An aggregated help page is at https://api.ibb.gov.tr/MetroIstanbul/Help. These are static/reference data — no train-position, no 'son tren' live ETA, no headway-realization feed. For a 'metro live' app, GetServiceStatus (disruptions) + GetTimeTable (scheduled) are the usable signals; actual live train location is NOT available.

findings.claim: The only real-time public transit feed is İETT's SOAP service for live BUS positions/trip realization; there is no equivalent for trains.
findings.detail: Dataset 'İETT Sefer Gerçekleşme Web Servisi' (slug sefer-gerceklesme-web-servisi), WSDL at https://api.ibb.gov.tr/iett/FiloDurum/SeferGerceklesme.asmx?wsdl, format SOAP, published by İETT, metadata_modified 2024-11-06. Description: 'aracın anlık olarak sefer gerçekleşme oranları paylaşılmaktadır' (real-time vehicle trip-realization). Ships with usage-doc PDFs (TR_İETT Web Servis Kullanım Dokümanı V.1.5, EN version). Common method GetHatOtoKonum_json(HatKodu) returns live bus coordinates per route. This is buses only — no metro/Marmaray train positions exist anywhere in the open data.

findings.claim: Rail line geometries and station points are available as fresh GeoJSON vector datasets (updated June 2025).
findings.detail: Three vector datasets, all metadata_modified 2025-06-05: 'Raylı Ulaşım Hatları Vektör Verisi' (rail lines, GeoJSON), 'Raylı Sistem İstasyon Noktaları Verisi' (station points, GeoJSON, slug rayli-sistem-istasyon-noktalari-verisi), and 'Raylı Ulaşım İstasyonları Vektör Verisi' (station areas/polygons, GeoJSON). Published by Ulaşım Dairesi Başkanlığı. These give map-ready line shapes + station coordinates and are far fresher than the stale GTFS shapes.csv — good basis for a map UI. Additional per-line info datasets exist (e.g. Kirazlı-Halkalı, Eminönü-Alibeyköy tram, Rumelihisarüstü-Aşiyan funicular).

findings.claim: The portal is a CKAN instance with a public, key-free read API (datastore + package APIs).
findings.detail: Base action API at https://data.ibb.gov.tr/en/api/3/action/ — package_show, package_search, datastore_search, datastore_search_sql all work without authentication for reads (write actions datastore_create/upsert require auth). Supports q (full-text), limit, offset, resource_id, and SQL via datastore_search_sql. Resource IDs double as DataStore table IDs (e.g. stops.csv resource_id d1f7c258-bbc1-406f-9ab2-7a7c1797c673). No documented rate limit found. Accessing datasets means accepting the İBB Açık Veri Lisansı + Kullanım Koşulları. An optional 'İBB Hesabım' login exists for the portal UI but is not required to read open data.

findings.claim: TCDD / Marmaray publishes NO official GTFS feed and NO public developer API of its own.
findings.detail: Searches for an official TCDD/Marmaray GTFS or developer API returned only: (a) schedule webpages on tcddtasimacilik.gov.tr (e.g. /marmaray/tr/gunluk_tren_saatleri, and ebilet.tcddtasimacilik.gov.tr/sefer-listesi for intercity), and (b) unofficial community scrapers on GitHub (rburaksaritas/tcdd-bilet-bulucu, birolemekli/tcdd-bilet-yer-kontrol, aliahmet/tcdd) that hit the e-bilet backend. The ONLY Marmaray data in GTFS form is inside the stale İBB 'Toplu Ulaşım GTFS Verisi' bundle (Marmaray slice dated 2022-05-22). So there is no authoritative, live, machine-readable Marmaray feed — a real gap for a metro-live app. (Negative result.)

findings.claim: İBB exposes additional aggregate/statistical rail datasets and other İETT web services beyond GTFS.
findings.detail: Aggregate (mostly CSV/XLSX, not real-time): 'Raylı Sistemler Günlük/Aylık/Yıllık Hat Bazlı Sefer Sayıları' (trip counts by line), 'Raylı Sistemlere Ait Hat Uzunluk Bilgileri' (line lengths), 'Raylı Sistemlere Ait Aktarma Bilgileri' (transfers), 'Metro Ve Tramvay Araçlarının Özellikleri' (rolling stock), 'Metro Hatları Enerji Tüketimi', 'Toplu Taşıma Bilet Fiyatları'. Other İETT API datasets: 'İETT Hat-Durak-Güzergâh Web Servisi', 'İETT Durak ve Hat Bilgileri Web Servisi', 'İETT Planlanan Sefer Saati Web Servisi' (scheduled departure times), 'İETT Duyurular Web Servisi' (announcements). ~41 datasets carry an API resource format; the IETT SOAP base is api.ibb.gov.tr/iett/.

findings.claim: Istanbul's İBB datasets are mirrored on national/other open-data portals, useful as fallbacks.
findings.detail: The same GTFS datasets appear on ulasav.csb.gov.tr (Türkiye Ulaşım/Çevre ve Şehircilik portal — e.g. /dataset/34-iett-gtfs-verisi, /dataset/34-public-transport-gtfs-data) and on opendata.b40cities.org (B40 Balkan cities network — e.g. /dataset/iett-gtfs-data). Transitland (transit.land/feeds) aggregates global GTFS/GTFS-RT and may carry an Istanbul feed. These are mirrors of the same (often stale) İBB data, not independent fresher sources, but provide redundancy.

dataSources.name: İBB Açık Veri Portalı (CKAN) — base action API
dataSources.type: REST API (CKAN: package_show, package_search, datastore_search, datastore_search_sql)
dataSources.access: free/open (no key for reads; accept İBB Open Data License)
dataSources.notes: Authoritative machine-readable index of all datasets below. datastore_search_sql allows SQL over CSV resources. Resource IDs = DataStore table IDs.

dataSources.name: Toplu Ulaşım GTFS Verisi (multimodal incl. Metro + Marmaray)
dataSources.notes: STALE/FROZEN: Metro slice 2023-01-09, Marmaray 2022-05-22; page says it will not be updated. Direct CSV download URLs under package id 121a9892-7945-419a-9b89-49f6083926df. Use for reference geometry only.

dataSources.name: İETT GTFS Verisi (bus + nostalgic tram)
dataSources.notes: Actively maintained (metadata_modified 2026-04-21). Package id 8540e256-6df5-4719-85bc-e64e91508ede. Bus-centric; rail relevance limited to nostalgic trams.

dataSources.name: Metro İstanbul REST web services (MetroMobile V2)
dataSources.access: free/open (no key observed); help at api.ibb.gov.tr/MetroIstanbul/Help
dataSources.notes: Endpoints: GetLines, GetStations, GetStationById/{LineId}, GetDirections, GetTimeTable, GetServiceStatus (live line up/down), GetMaps, GetTicketPrice/TR, GetRailwayGroups, GetActivities, GetLineProjects, GetFailureTypes. STATIC reference + service status only — NO train positions.

dataSources.name: İETT Sefer Gerçekleşme (real-time bus positions)
dataSources.access: free/open; usage-doc PDFs (TR/EN V.1.5) on the dataset page
dataSources.notes: The only real-time transit feed in Istanbul open data — BUSES ONLY. Method e.g. GetHatOtoKonum_json(HatKodu) -> live coords. Dataset: data.ibb.gov.tr/dataset/sefer-gerceklesme-web-servisi

dataSources.name: Raylı Ulaşım Hatları / İstasyon Noktaları Vektör Verisi
dataSources.type: GeoJSON (vector lines + station points + station areas)
dataSources.notes: Fresh (2025-06-05). Three datasets: rayli-ulasim-hatlari-vektor-verisi (lines), rayli-sistem-istasyon-noktalari-verisi (point stations), rayli-ulasim-istasyonlari-vektor-verisi (station areas). Best source for map-ready rail geometry/coordinates.

dataSources.name: İETT other web services (hat-durak-güzergah, planlanan sefer, duyurular)
dataSources.notes: İETT Hat-Durak-Güzergâh, İETT Planlanan Sefer Saati (scheduled departures), İETT Duyurular (announcements). SOAP base https://api.ibb.gov.tr/iett/. ~41 API-format datasets total on the portal.

dataSources.name: TCDD Taşımacılık Marmaray schedule pages (no API)
dataSources.access: free but unofficial for programmatic use; no GTFS/API
dataSources.notes: No official Marmaray GTFS or developer API exists. ebilet.tcddtasimacilik.gov.tr backend is used by unofficial GitHub scrapers (rburaksaritas/tcdd-bilet-bulucu, birolemekli/tcdd-bilet-yer-kontrol). Only GTFS for Marmaray is the stale İBB bundle.

dataSources.name: ulasav.csb.gov.tr & opendata.b40cities.org mirrors; Transitland
dataSources.notes: Mirror copies of the İBB GTFS (same staleness). Transitland (transit.land/feeds) may host an Istanbul GTFS feed for aggregation. Redundancy, not fresher data.

dataSources.name: Community wrappers (unofficial)
dataSources.type: Open-source code (Node/Express GeoJSON wrapper over İETT SOAP), Python clients
dataSources.notes: Examples: hakanatak/dataibbgovtr (SOAP->GeoJSON), AydinAdn/IBB.Api (.NET client for İETT/İBB services). Useful as integration references for the SOAP services.

openQuestions: Is there ANY real-time train/metro position or per-train ETA feed (even internal/undocumented) behind the Metro İstanbul mobile app? The public MetroMobile V2 API only exposes static data + GetServiceStatus; the app may use a private endpoint worth network-inspecting.
Does GetServiceStatus update frequently enough and with enough granularity (per-line vs per-segment disruptions) to drive a 'live' status indicator?

Will the frozen multimodal GTFS ('will not be updated') ever be superseded? Is the current Metro/Marmaray schedule reconstructable from GetTimeTable (which returns metro timetables but may not be GTFS-shaped or include Marmaray)?
Exact rate limits / fair-use for api.ibb.gov.tr REST endpoints and the SeferGerceklesme SOAP service — not documented publicly; needs empirical testing or contacting İBB.

Does Marmaray (TCDD) timetable data have a stable, scrapeable structure on tcddtasimacilik.gov.tr, and are the ToS compatible with redistribution in an app? No official feed means scraping or manual entry is the only path.
Is an Istanbul GTFS-Realtime feed registered on Transitland or anywhere, or is GTFS-RT entirely absent for Istanbul rail?
Whether GetStations/GetStationById return WGS84 lat/lon (to avoid needing the separate GeoJSON datasets) and how station IDs map between the REST API, the GTFS feed, and the GeoJSON vector data.
